patterns.name: Opposite Ends (Left + Right)
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: Place one pointer at the very start of the array and another at the very end, then move them toward each other based on comparing the values (or characters) they currently point to. Because both pointers only advance inward, the total work stays proportional to the array's length, turning an O(n^2) pairwise search into a single O(n) pass. It works best on sorted arrays, or whenever the decision at each step only depends on the two outer boundaries rather than the full ordering. The scan stops once the two pointers meet or cross.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Finding a pair in a sorted array whose sum matches a target
Checking whether a sequence reads the same forwards and backwards
Maximizing the area or capacity trapped between two boundaries
Finding a triplet of elements that satisfies a sum condition
Reversing a sequence in place without extra memory

steps: Set left = 0 and right = length of the array minus 1
While left is less than right, repeat the following
Evaluate the condition using the values at left and right (e.g. their sum, or whether they match)
If the condition is satisfied, record or return the result
If the current value is too small, move left forward by one to increase it
If the current value is too large, move right backward by one to decrease it
Stop when left meets or passes right, and return the final result (or 'not found')

variations.name: Pair sum equals target
variations.summary: Two pointers move inward on a sorted array until their sum matches a target
variations.movement: Both ends → inward
variations.recognizeIt: The array is already sorted and the task asks for a pair whose sum hits a target exactly; moving a pointer inward changes the sum in a known direction, so nested loops become unnecessary.
variations.keyInsight: Because the array is sorted, incrementing left can only increase the sum and decrementing right can only decrease it, so no valid pair is ever skipped.

variations.name: Maximum area between two walls
variations.summary: Keep the taller wall fixed and move the shorter wall inward to search for a bigger container
variations.movement: Shorter side → inward
variations.recognizeIt: The question asks to maximize an area bounded by two chosen positions in an array of heights; the area is always limited by the shorter of the two walls, so moving the taller one can never help.
variations.keyInsight: Moving the taller wall inward only shrinks the width while the height stays capped by the shorter wall, so it can never increase the area — only moving the shorter wall has a chance to help.

variations.name: Palindrome check with inward comparison
variations.summary: Compare characters from both ends moving inward, skipping characters that don't count
variations.movement: Both ends → inward
variations.recognizeIt: The task is to confirm a sequence reads identically forwards and backwards; comparing outer characters and moving both pointers inward avoids building a reversed copy.

variations.name: Triplet sum via fixed element plus inward pointers
variations.summary: Fix one element in a loop, then use opposite-ends pointers on the remaining sorted slice to find the other two
variations.movement: Outer loop fixes one end; inner two pointers move inward
variations.recognizeIt: The problem wants combinations of three (not two) elements meeting a sum condition; fixing the first element turns it back into the familiar two-pointer pair-sum problem on the rest of the array.
variations.keyInsight: Sorting first turns the triplet search into repeated pair-sum searches, and skipping over repeated values at each pointer is what keeps the output free of duplicate triplets.

patterns.name: Same Direction (Fast & Slow)
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: Two pointers start at (or near) the beginning of the array and move in the same direction, but at different rates or under different rules — a 'slow' pointer that only advances when a useful element is found, and a 'fast' pointer that scans every element. This rewrites the array in place in a single left-to-right pass, keeping the elements worth keeping packed at the front while the fast pointer explores ahead. It's the standard technique for compacting or filtering a sequence without extra memory.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Removing duplicate values from a sorted array in place
Removing all occurrences of a specific value from an array
Moving zeros (or any marked elements) to the end while keeping order
Detecting a cycle when array values are treated as pointers to other indices
Compacting an array to keep only elements that satisfy some condition

steps: Set slow = 0 (marks where the next kept element should go) and fast = 0
Move fast forward one step at a time through the array
At each fast position, check whether the current element should be kept
If it should be kept, place or swap it at the slow index and advance slow by one
Continue until fast reaches the end of the array
Return slow as the new logical length, or the array up to slow

variations.name: Remove duplicates in place (sorted array)
variations.summary: slow marks the last unique element written; fast scans ahead for the next different value
variations.movement: Both same direction; fast ahead of slow
variations.recognizeIt: The array is sorted and duplicates must be collapsed in place; since duplicates in a sorted array are always adjacent, the slow write-pointer only needs to advance when fast finds a genuinely new value.
variations.keyInsight: Comparing against nums[slow] (the last kept value) rather than nums[fast-1] is what keeps this correct even across long runs of repeats.

variations.name: Remove all instances of a value in place
variations.summary: fast scans every element; slow only advances and writes when the element should be kept
variations.inputShape: Unsorted array plus a value to remove
variations.movement: Both same direction; fast ahead of slow
variations.recognizeIt: The task asks to strip out every occurrence of a given value in place and report the new length; unlike the sorted-duplicates case, the array doesn't need to be sorted, just filtered.

variations.name: Move zeros to the end in place
variations.summary: fast scans for non-zero elements; slow marks where the next non-zero should be written
variations.movement: Both same direction; fast ahead of slow
variations.recognizeIt: The task wants all zeros pushed to the back while keeping the relative order of everything else — a filtering pass and a padding step, both driven by the same fast/slow scan.
variations.keyInsight: Swapping (instead of just overwriting) keeps the operation reversible in a single pass, so the zeros end up in the tail automatically without a separate cleanup step.

variations.name: Cycle detection on array-as-linked-list
variations.summary: Treat each value as a pointer to the next index, then use Floyd's fast/slow cycle detection to find the entry point of the cycle, which is the duplicate
variations.inputShape: Array of n+1 integers, each between 1 and n
variations.movement: slow advances by 1 index-hop, fast advances by 2 index-hops
variations.recognizeIt: The array is described as containing exactly one repeated value among a range of n+1 numbers in n slots — reading each value as 'the index to jump to next' turns the array into a linked list with a guaranteed cycle at the duplicate.
variations.keyInsight: This is Floyd's cycle-detection trick repurposed for arrays: the meeting point of the two phases is mathematically guaranteed to be the cycle's entry point, which corresponds to the duplicate value.

patterns.name: Partition / Dutch National Flag
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: A three-way partitioning technique using three markers — low, mid, and high — that classify every element into one of three buckets (less-than, equal-to, greater-than a pivot) while making only one pass through the array. Elements already classified as 'low' or 'high' are pushed to their respective ends via swaps, while 'mid' simply advances past correctly-placed elements. It's named after Dijkstra's Dutch national flag problem, where three colors of balls need to be sorted into three contiguous bands.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Sorting an array that only contains a small, fixed set of distinct values
Partitioning an array around a pivot value as a subroutine for quicksort or quickselect
Grouping array elements into two or three categories in a single in-place pass
Rearranging elements so a chosen value's final position matches its sorted position

steps: Set low = 0, mid = 0, and high = length of the array minus 1
While mid is less than or equal to high, repeat the following
If arr[mid] belongs in the 'low' group, swap arr[low] with arr[mid], then advance both low and mid
If arr[mid] belongs in the 'mid' group, just advance mid
If arr[mid] belongs in the 'high' group, swap arr[mid] with arr[high] and decrement high, but don't advance mid yet (the newly swapped-in value still needs classifying)
Stop once mid passes high; the array is now partitioned into three contiguous bands

variations.name: Sort three fixed values (0, 1, 2) in place
variations.summary: A direct application of the three-way partition where the pivot is the middle value (1)
variations.inputShape: Array containing only the values 0, 1, and 2
variations.movement: low/high converge inward, mid sweeps left to right
variations.recognizeIt: The array is guaranteed to contain only a small fixed set of values (here, three) and needs sorting in one pass without extra space — a strong signal for Dutch National Flag over a general sort.
variations.keyInsight: Using 1 as the pivot naturally splits the array into 0s, 1s, and 2s without needing to know how many of each value exist ahead of time.

variations.name: Two-way partition (segregate 0s and 1s)
variations.summary: A simpler two-bucket version using only a write pointer and a scan pointer, since there's no 'equal' group to track
variations.inputShape: Array containing only two distinct values
variations.movement: left advances on match, right/scan sweeps forward
variations.recognizeIt: Only two distinct values are involved, so the middle band collapses away and a single write-pointer swapping against a scan pointer is enough — this is the two-color special case of the three-color problem.

variations.name: Partition around a pivot for quickselect
variations.summary: Use the same classification idea to place a chosen pivot at its final sorted position, then recurse into only the needed side
variations.inputShape: Unsorted array plus a target rank (e.g. kth largest)
variations.movement: low/high converge inward around a chosen pivot value
variations.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra (iterative), O(log n) if recursive
variations.recognizeIt: The question only needs the kth smallest/largest element, not a fully sorted array — partitioning around a pivot and recursing into just one side (like quicksort, but only one branch) avoids sorting everything.
variations.keyInsight: Recursing into only the side that contains the target rank (instead of both sides, like quicksort does) is what keeps the average case linear rather than n log n.

patterns.name: Fixed Size
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: Maintain a window of a constant, pre-determined size that slides one step at a time across the array. Instead of recomputing the window's aggregate (sum, max, character count, etc.) from scratch at every position, the window is updated incrementally: subtract whatever is leaving on the left, add whatever is entering on the right. This turns what looks like an O(n*k) brute-force scan into a single O(n) pass.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Finding the maximum or minimum sum of any contiguous subarray of a fixed length k
Computing a moving average or running aggregate over the last k elements
Finding the maximum or minimum value in every window of size k
Finding a specific element (e.g. the first negative number) in every window of size k
Detecting an anagram or permutation of one string inside another

steps: Compute the aggregate (e.g. sum) of the first k elements to initialize the window
Record this as the current best/answer
Slide the window forward one position at a time: subtract the element leaving on the left, add the element entering on the right
Update the current aggregate with this one incremental change instead of recomputing it from scratch
Compare or update the best answer using the new aggregate
Repeat until the window reaches the end of the array
stepByStepAlgorithm.code:
def fixed_window(arr, k):
    window_sum = sum(arr[:k])
    best = window_sum
    for i in range(k, len(arr)):
        window_sum += arr[i] - arr[i - k]  # slide: add new, remove old
        best = max(best, window_sum)
    return best

variations.name: Maximum sum subarray of size k
variations.summary: Slide a window of fixed length k, updating the running sum incrementally instead of recomputing it
variations.inputShape: Array of numbers plus a fixed window length k
variations.movement: Window slides right one step at a time
variations.recognizeIt: The window length k is fixed and given up front, and the task only cares about a running aggregate (sum) over that fixed span — the giveaway is a constant k rather than a size that grows or shrinks based on a condition.
variations.code:
def max_sum_subarray(arr, k):
    window_sum = sum(arr[:k])
    best = window_sum
    for i in range(k, len(arr)):
        window_sum += arr[i] - arr[i - k]
        best = max(best, window_sum)
    return best
variations.keyInsight: Recomputing the sum of each window from scratch would cost O(k) per position; updating it by one addition and one subtraction is what brings the total cost down to O(n).

variations.name: First negative number in every window
variations.summary: Maintain a queue of indices of negative numbers currently inside the window; the front of the queue is always the answer for that window
variations.inputShape: Array of numbers plus a fixed window length k
variations.movement: Window slides right one step at a time
variations.recognizeIt: The task wants a specific element (the first negative) reported for every window of a fixed size — a queue that only stores candidate indices and discards ones that have slid out of range answers each window in amortized O(1) time.
variations.keyInsight: Only negative indices are ever stored, so the queue never grows large — most elements are simply skipped, and each index enters and leaves the queue at most once.

variations.name: Maximum in every window of size k
variations.summary: Maintain a monotonically decreasing deque of indices so the front always holds the current window's maximum
variations.inputShape: Array of numbers plus a fixed window length k
variations.movement: Window slides right one step at a time
variations.recognizeIt: The task wants the maximum (not just sum) for every fixed-size window; because a plain running aggregate can't 'un-max' when the max slides out, a monotonic deque that discards smaller elements from the back is needed.
variations.keyInsight: Any element smaller than a later element still inside the window can never become the max while that later element remains, so it's safe to discard it permanently from the back of the deque.

variations.name: Find all anagram start indices in a string
variations.summary: Slide a window the same length as the pattern, tracking character counts, and compare the window's count to the pattern's count
variations.inputShape: A string plus a shorter pattern string
variations.movement: Window slides right one step at a time
variations.recognizeIt: The window length is fixed by the pattern's length, and 'anagram' means a character-frequency match rather than a numeric aggregate — the same fixed-window slide, but what's maintained incrementally is a count table.
variations.keyInsight: Comparing two Counter objects is still proportional to alphabet size, not window size, so each slide stays effectively constant time rather than re-scanning the whole window.

patterns.name: Variable Size
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: Unlike the fixed-size window, here both boundaries move independently: the right pointer expands the window by scanning forward, and the left pointer contracts it whenever the window violates (or, for maximization, no longer needs) some constraint. The window grows and shrinks dynamically in response to the data, and each element is added and removed from the window at most once, keeping the whole scan O(n) despite there being no fixed length.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Finding the shortest subarray or substring meeting a condition
Finding the longest subarray or substring meeting a condition (e.g. at most or exactly k distinct characters)
Finding the smallest window in a string containing all characters of another string
Counting or bounding subarrays whose sum stays within some threshold

steps: Initialize left = 0 and a running aggregate (sum, counts, etc.) representing an empty window
Move right forward one step at a time, folding arr[right] into the window's aggregate
While the window satisfies (or violates) the condition being checked, adjust by shrinking from the left: remove arr[left] from the aggregate and advance left
After adjusting, update the best answer using the current window's size or contents
Continue until right reaches the end of the array
Return the best answer found
stepByStepAlgorithm.code:
def variable_window(arr, target):
    left = 0
    window_sum = 0
    best = float('inf')
    for right in range(len(arr)):
        window_sum += arr[right]
        while window_sum >= target:
            best = min(best, right - left + 1)
            window_sum -= arr[left]
            left += 1
    return best if best != float('inf') else 0

variations.name: Shortest subarray with sum at least target
variations.summary: Expand the window until its sum meets the target, then shrink from the left as far as possible while it still qualifies
variations.inputShape: Array of positive numbers plus a target sum
variations.movement: right expands, left contracts while condition holds
variations.recognizeIt: The task wants the smallest/shortest window satisfying a sum condition rather than a fixed length — 'smallest subarray such that...' is the classic tell for shrink-while-valid.
variations.code:
def min_subarray_len(target, nums):
    left = 0
    window_sum = 0
    best = float('inf')
    for right in range(len(nums)):
        window_sum += nums[right]
        while window_sum >= target:
            best = min(best, right - left + 1)
            window_sum -= nums[left]
            left += 1
    return best if best != float('inf') else 0
variations.keyInsight: Because all numbers are positive, shrinking the window can only ever decrease the sum, so once the sum dips below target it's safe to stop shrinking and go back to expanding.

variations.name: Longest substring without repeating characters
variations.summary: Expand the window by adding characters; when a repeat is found, jump the left pointer forward past it
variations.movement: right expands, left jumps forward on violation
variations.recognizeIt: The task wants the longest window satisfying a uniqueness condition — track the last-seen position of each character to jump left forward instead of shrinking one step at a time when a repeat shows up.
variations.keyInsight: Jumping left directly to one past the repeated character's last position (instead of incrementing left one at a time) avoids redundant work and keeps the pass linear.

variations.name: Longest substring with exactly k distinct characters
variations.summary: Expand the window and track character counts; shrink from the left whenever the distinct count exceeds k
variations.movement: right expands, left contracts while distinct count exceeds k
variations.recognizeIt: The task fixes a target number of distinct characters (not a numeric sum) and asks for the longest window meeting it — a frequency map sized to the window, shrunk whenever it holds too many distinct keys.
variations.keyInsight: Checking len(counts) == k only after shrinking is what distinguishes 'exactly k' from 'at most k' — a window with fewer than k distinct characters simply doesn't update the answer yet.

variations.name: Smallest window containing all characters of another string
variations.summary: Expand the window until it contains every required character (with correct counts), then shrink from the left as far as it stays valid
variations.inputShape: Two strings: the search string and the pattern to cover
variations.movement: right expands, left contracts while the window still satisfies the pattern
variations.recognizeIt: The task wants the smallest window covering all characters (with multiplicity) of a second string — tracking how many required characters are currently 'satisfied' lets validity be checked in O(1) rather than rescanning counts each time.
variations.keyInsight: Tracking a single 'missing' counter (how many required characters are still unmet) turns the validity check into O(1), instead of comparing entire frequency maps at every position.

patterns.name: Prefix Sum
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: Precompute a running total array where each position holds the sum of all elements up to that index. Once built, the sum of any subarray [i, j] can be answered in O(1) by subtracting two prefix values, instead of re-summing the range every time. This trades one O(n) preprocessing pass for O(1) range-sum queries afterward, and pairs naturally with a hashmap when the question is about counting or finding sums rather than just answering fixed ranges.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Answering many 'sum of subarray from i to j' queries efficiently
Counting how many subarrays sum to an exact target
Finding the equilibrium/pivot index where the left sum equals the right sum
Detecting a subarray whose sum is divisible by some number

steps: Create a prefix array of length n+1, with prefix[0] = 0
For each index i from 0 to n-1, set prefix[i+1] = prefix[i] + arr[i]
To get the sum of the subarray from index i to j inclusive, compute prefix[j+1] - prefix[i]
For queries about counting or finding a target sum, maintain a hashmap of prefix sums seen so far instead of a plain array
At each step, look up prefix_sum - target in that hashmap to check whether some earlier prefix qualifies
Update the hashmap with the current prefix sum after each step

variations.name: Answer many range-sum queries in O(1)
variations.summary: Precompute prefix sums once, then answer each range-sum query by subtracting two prefix values
variations.inputShape: Static array plus many (i, j) range queries
variations.movement: One forward pass to build the prefix array; O(1) lookup per query afterward
variations.timeComplexity: O(n) to build, O(1) per query
variations.recognizeIt: The array doesn't change and there will be many range-sum queries — precomputing once and answering each query in constant time beats re-summing the range every time.

variations.name: Count subarrays summing to k
variations.summary: Track how many times each prefix sum has occurred; a match of (current prefix - k) means a subarray ending here sums to k
variations.inputShape: Array of integers (can include negatives) plus a target sum k
variations.movement: One forward pass, using a hashmap of prefix-sum counts
variations.recognizeIt: The array may contain negative numbers, which rules out a sliding window; counting subarrays with an exact sum is the classic sign to pair prefix sums with a hashmap of counts seen so far.
variations.code:
def subarray_sum(nums, k):
    count = 0
    prefix_sum = 0
    seen = {0: 1}  # empty prefix occurs once
    for num in nums:
        prefix_sum += num
        count += seen.get(prefix_sum - k, 0)
        seen[prefix_sum] = seen.get(prefix_sum, 0) + 1
    return count
variations.keyInsight: Seeding the hashmap with {0: 1} accounts for subarrays that start at index 0 and still sum to exactly k.

variations.name: Find the equilibrium/pivot index
variations.summary: Compute the total sum once, then walk the array tracking the running left sum and derive the right sum by subtraction
variations.movement: One forward pass
variations.recognizeIt: The task wants a single index where the sum on one side equals the sum on the other — computing the total sum up front lets the right-side sum be derived instead of recomputed at each index.
variations.keyInsight: Deriving right_sum as total - left_sum - num avoids ever summing the right side directly, keeping the whole check O(1) per index.

variations.name: Subarray sum divisible by k
variations.summary: Track prefix sums modulo k; two indices sharing the same remainder mean the subarray between them is divisible by k
variations.inputShape: Array of integers plus a divisor k
variations.movement: One forward pass, using a hashmap of remainder-to-earliest-index
variations.recognizeIt: The condition is divisibility rather than an exact sum — reducing each prefix sum modulo k and looking for a repeated remainder is what detects a subarray whose sum is a multiple of k.
variations.keyInsight: Only the first occurrence of each remainder is kept, since any later occurrence at the same remainder already guarantees a long-enough divisible subarray between them.

patterns.name: Prefix Sum
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: Prefix Sum precomputes cumulative totals of an array so the sum of any contiguous range can be answered in constant time instead of re-summing the range on every query. You build a running-total array once, then answer each range-sum query as a subtraction of two precomputed values. The same running-total idea extends beyond static range queries: paired with a hashmap that tracks how often each running total has occurred, it can count or locate subarrays whose sum matches a target in a single pass.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Answering many range-sum queries on a static array efficiently
Finding an index where the left sum equals the right sum (pivot/equilibrium index)
Counting or finding subarrays whose sum equals a target
Converting an O(n) per-query brute force into O(1) per query after O(n) preprocessing
Detecting zero-sum subarrays
Building a running total array in one pass

steps: Create a prefix array of size n+1, with a leading 0 so range sums never need a special case for left = 0.
Iterate through the original array once, setting prefix[i+1] = prefix[i] + arr[i].
To answer a range sum query [left, right], compute prefix[right+1] - prefix[left].
For problems needing only a running total (pivot index, counting subarrays with sum k), keep a single running variable instead of storing the full prefix array.

variations.name: Static Range Sum Queries
variations.summary: Precompute once, answer each range-sum query in O(1)
variations.movement: One forward precompute pass, then O(1) lookups
variations.timeComplexity: O(1) per query after O(n) build
variations.recognizeIt: Multiple range-sum queries on an array that never changes between queries. If the array were mutable between queries you'd reach for a Fenwick tree/segment tree instead.
variations.keyInsight: prefix[r+1] - prefix[l] removes everything before index l, leaving exactly the sum of [l, r].

variations.name: Pivot / Equilibrium Index
variations.summary: Find the index where the left-side sum equals the right-side sum
variations.movement: Left-to-right, tracking a running left sum against the known total
variations.recognizeIt: Wording like 'sum of elements to the left equals sum to the right' — no queries, just one index to find.
variations.keyInsight: You never need a real prefix array here — total - left_sum - num gives the right-side sum without a second pass.

variations.name: Count Subarrays with Sum Equal to K
variations.summary: Track running sum and a hashmap of seen prefix sums to count subarrays matching a target sum
variations.inputShape: Single array (may include negatives), one target k
variations.movement: Single forward pass with hashmap lookups
variations.recognizeIt: 'Count/find subarrays that sum to k' where the array can contain negative numbers — negatives rule out the sliding-window approach, which needs the hashmap trick instead.
variations.keyInsight: If prefixSum[i] - k == prefixSum[j] for some earlier j, the subarray between j+1 and i sums to exactly k.

variations.name: Running Sum Construction
variations.summary: Build and return the running total array itself, rather than answering queries against it
variations.movement: Single forward pass
variations.recognizeIt: The output itself is the prefix array — no queries, no target, just 'return the running sum'.
variations.code:
def running_sum(nums):
    result = []
    total = 0
    for num in nums:
        total += num
        result.append(total)
    return result

patterns.name: Prefix XOR
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: Prefix XOR mirrors Prefix Sum but uses XOR's self-cancelling property instead of subtraction. You precompute a running XOR of all elements up to each index; the XOR of any range [l, r] is then prefix[r+1] ^ prefix[l], because XOR-ing the shared prefix twice cancels it out. The same cancellation trick, paired with a hashmap, can count subarrays whose XOR equals a target, and underlies several 'find the missing/unique element' tricks.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Answering many range-XOR queries on a static array efficiently
Counting or finding subarrays whose XOR equals a target
Finding a missing number from a range without extra space
Any problem that leans on a^a=0 and a^0=a to cancel out paired values
Converting an O(n) per-query brute force into O(1) per query after O(n) preprocessing

steps: Create a prefix array of size n+1, with a leading 0 so range XORs never need a special case for left = 0.
Iterate through the original array once, setting prefix[i+1] = prefix[i] ^ arr[i].
To answer a range XOR query [left, right], compute prefix[right+1] ^ prefix[left].
For problems needing only a running XOR (missing number, counting subarrays with XOR k), keep a single running variable instead of storing the full prefix array.

variations.name: Range XOR Query (Immutable)
variations.summary: Precompute prefix XOR once, answer each range XOR query in O(1)
variations.movement: One forward precompute pass, then O(1) lookups
variations.timeComplexity: O(1) per query after O(n) build
variations.recognizeIt: Multiple range-XOR queries on a static array — the phrase 'XOR of elements between indices' repeated across a query list is the giveaway.
variations.keyInsight: XOR is its own inverse, so prefix[r+1] ^ prefix[l] cancels out everything before index l, leaving only the XOR of [l, r].

variations.name: Count Subarrays with XOR Equal to K
variations.summary: Track running XOR and a hashmap of seen prefix XOR values to count subarrays matching a target XOR
variations.movement: Single forward pass with hashmap lookups
variations.recognizeIt: 'Count/find subarrays whose XOR equals k' — structurally identical to subarray-sum-equals-k but with XOR replacing subtraction.
variations.keyInsight: If prefixXor[i] ^ k == prefixXor[j] for some earlier j, the subarray between j+1 and i has XOR exactly k, since XOR-ing prefixXor[j] again cancels the shared prefix.

variations.name: Missing Number via XOR
variations.summary: XOR the full expected range with the array elements; every present number cancels out, leaving only the missing one
variations.inputShape: Array of n distinct numbers from a known contiguous range with exactly one missing
variations.movement: Two linear passes (or one combined), no indexing needed
variations.recognizeIt: Exactly one number missing from a known range, with a hint at doing it in O(1) extra space — that's the XOR-cancellation signal rather than a sum-based approach (which risks overflow).
variations.keyInsight: a^a=0 and a^0=a, so XOR-ing every expected value with every present value cancels all matched pairs, leaving only the unmatched (missing) number.

patterns.name: 2D Prefix Sum
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: 2D Prefix Sum extends the 1D idea to matrices: precompute a cumulative-sum grid so that the sum of any rectangular submatrix can be answered in O(1) using inclusion-exclusion. Each cell of the prefix grid stores the sum of everything above and to the left of it. To get the sum of an arbitrary rectangle, you take the sum up to its bottom-right corner and subtract the two overlapping strips above and to the left, then add back the corner that got subtracted twice.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Answering many rectangular submatrix sum queries on a static matrix efficiently
Computing a fixed-radius block sum centered at every cell in a matrix
Finding the maximum submatrix sum under a bound constraint
Image-processing style integral image / summed-area table computations
Converting an O(rows*cols) per-query brute force into O(1) per query after one preprocessing pass

steps: Create a prefix grid of size (rows+1) x (cols+1), with a leading zero row and column so no boundary index ever goes negative.
Fill it row by row: prefix[i+1][j+1] = matrix[i][j] + prefix[i][j+1] + prefix[i+1][j] - prefix[i][j], where the subtraction avoids double-counting the overlapping top-left region.
To answer the sum of rectangle (row1, col1) to (row2, col2), compute prefix[row2+1][col2+1] - prefix[row1][col2+1] - prefix[row2+1][col1] + prefix[row1][col1].
Reuse the same prefix grid for every subsequent query without rebuilding it.

variations.name: Static Submatrix Sum Queries
variations.summary: Precompute once, answer each submatrix sum query in O(1)
variations.inputShape: Fixed 2D matrix, multiple (row1, col1, row2, col2) queries
variations.movement: One O(rows*cols) precompute pass, then O(1) inclusion-exclusion lookups
variations.timeComplexity: O(1) per query after O(rows*cols) build
variations.recognizeIt: Multiple 'sum of this rectangle' queries on a matrix that never changes between queries — the direct 2D analogue of static-range-sum.
variations.keyInsight: Inclusion-exclusion: subtract both the row-above and column-left rectangles, then add back the corner region that got subtracted twice.

variations.name: Matrix Block Sum
variations.summary: Compute a k-radius block sum centered at every cell in one pass, clamping at matrix edges
variations.inputShape: Full matrix plus radius k, output is a same-shaped matrix of block sums
variations.movement: Build the prefix grid once, then O(1) inclusion-exclusion per output cell
variations.recognizeIt: 'For every cell, sum the surrounding window clamped to the matrix edges' — an entire output matrix of local sums rather than a handful of ad-hoc queries.
variations.keyInsight: Clamping the row/col bounds with max/min before applying the same inclusion-exclusion formula handles edge and corner cells without any special-casing.

variations.name: Max Sum Rectangle No Larger Than K
variations.summary: Fix a pair of row boundaries, collapse to a 1D column-sum array, then use a sorted running-prefix search to find the best submatrix sum <= k
variations.inputShape: Full matrix plus one bound k
variations.movement: O(rows^2) row-pair loop, each doing an O(cols log cols) sorted-prefix scan
variations.recognizeIt: 'Best submatrix sum not exceeding k' — the upper-bound constraint on k, rather than a plain maximum, signals this heavier row-pair-collapse-plus-sorted-search technique instead of a direct prefix-sum lookup.
variations.keyInsight: For a fixed top/bottom row pair, the problem collapses to the 1D 'best subarray sum <= k' problem, solvable by keeping seen prefix sums in a sorted structure and binary-searching for the tightest fit.

patterns.name: Maximum Subarray Sum
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: Kadane's algorithm finds the maximum-sum contiguous subarray in a single linear pass by making one local decision per element: either extend the running subarray by including the current element, or abandon everything before it and restart fresh at the current element — whichever produces a larger running sum. It avoids the O(n^2) brute force of checking every possible subarray by only ever tracking the best sum ending at the current index and the best sum seen so far overall.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Finding the maximum-sum contiguous subarray in an array that contains negative numbers
Finding the maximum-sum subarray when the array wraps around (circular)
Recovering the actual start/end indices of the best subarray, not just its sum
Extending to 2D for maximum-sum submatrix problems
Correctly handling the all-negative edge case

steps: Initialize current_sum and best_sum to the value of the first element.
For each subsequent element, set current_sum = max(element, current_sum + element) — either extend the running subarray or restart at this element.
After updating current_sum, set best_sum = max(best_sum, current_sum).
Return best_sum once the array is exhausted.

variations.name: Classic Kadane's (Max Sum Only)
variations.summary: Standard single-pass max-sum contiguous subarray
variations.movement: Left to right, one extend-or-restart decision per element
variations.recognizeIt: 'Find the contiguous subarray with the largest sum' with no other constraints — the canonical Kadane's setup.
variations.keyInsight: A running sum that has gone negative can never help any future subarray, so the moment extending would do worse than the current element alone, restarting there is always at least as good.

variations.name: Kadane's with Boundary Tracking
variations.summary: Same extend-or-restart rule as classic Kadane's, but also tracks the start/end indices of the best subarray found
variations.movement: Left to right, updating boundary pointers alongside the running sum
variations.recognizeIt: The problem asks for the subarray itself (its elements or indices), not just its sum — the only difference from classic Kadane's is the extra bookkeeping.
variations.keyInsight: Only advance the 'start' candidate pointer when an actual restart happens, and only commit it into the answer's boundaries when a new best_sum is found — this keeps the reported range correct even across several restarts.

variations.name: Maximum Sum Circular Subarray
variations.summary: Handle wrap-around by comparing normal Kadane's max against (total sum − Kadane's minimum subarray sum)
variations.inputShape: Single circular array (wrap-around allowed), may contain negatives
variations.movement: Two linear passes: standard Kadane's for the max, an inverted Kadane's for the min
variations.recognizeIt: 'Circular array' or 'the subarray may wrap around from the end back to the start' in the problem statement — that phrase signals you need the total-minus-minimum candidate alongside the ordinary max.
variations.keyInsight: If every element is negative, the wrap-around candidate (total - best_min) would incorrectly represent an empty subarray, so that case must fall back to the plain non-circular best_max instead.

patterns.name: Maximum Product Subarray
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: Unlike sum, multiplying by a negative number can flip a large negative running product into the largest positive one seen so far. Because of this, you can't track just a single running best the way Kadane's does for sums — you must track both a running maximum and a running minimum product at every step, since the minimum (often the most negative value) can become the new maximum the instant it's multiplied by another negative number. Zeros act as natural reset points, since any product touching a zero collapses to zero.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Finding the maximum-product contiguous subarray in an array containing negative numbers and zeros
Counting contiguous subarrays whose running product stays under a target bound
Handling sign flips correctly when negative numbers appear an odd or even number of times within a window
Adapting Kadane's-style single-pass thinking from addition to multiplication

steps: Initialize current_max, current_min, and best all to the first element.
For each subsequent number, if it is negative, swap current_max and current_min first, since multiplying by a negative flips which one would become larger.
Update current_max = max(num, current_max * num) and current_min = min(num, current_min * num) — either extend the running product or restart at the current element.
Update best = max(best, current_max) after every step.
Return best once the array is exhausted.

variations.name: Classic Maximum Product Subarray
variations.summary: Standard single pass tracking both a running max and running min product
variations.inputShape: Single array, may contain negatives and zeros
variations.movement: Left to right, swap-then-update per element
variations.recognizeIt: 'Find the contiguous subarray with the largest product' — the product analogue of classic Kadane's, flagged by the word 'product' instead of 'sum'.
variations.keyInsight: A negative number multiplied by the current running minimum (which may itself be negative) can become the new maximum, so both running extremes must be tracked, not just one.

variations.name: Count Subarrays with Product Less Than K
variations.summary: A shrinking sliding window (not the max/min swap trick) counting contiguous subarrays whose product stays below k
variations.inputShape: Single array of strictly positive integers, one bound k
variations.movement: Two pointers, shrink window from the left whenever the running product reaches k
variations.recognizeIt: 'Count subarrays whose product is less than k' combined with a guarantee that all elements are positive — positivity is exactly what allows a monotonic shrinking window instead of Kadane's negative-number handling.
variations.keyInsight: Because every element is positive, the running product only grows as the window widens, so a simple shrink-from-the-left sliding window works without any of the sign-flip bookkeeping the classic variation needs.

patterns.name: Subarray with Given Sum / XOR
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: This pattern locates — rather than just counts — a contiguous subarray whose sum or XOR matches a target value. When every element is non-negative, a two-pointer window can grow and shrink monotonically to home in on the target sum directly. Once negative numbers are allowed, the running sum is no longer monotonic, so the search instead tracks prefix values (sum or XOR) in a hashmap and looks up when the required earlier prefix was first seen — the same core idea used to count matching subarrays, but repurposed to recover the subarray's actual boundaries or maximum length.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Locating a contiguous subarray whose sum equals a target value
Finding the longest subarray whose sum equals a target, including arrays with negative numbers
Finding the longest subarray whose XOR equals a target value
Distinguishing when a monotonic sliding window is valid (non-negative elements) versus when a prefix hashmap is required (negatives allowed)

steps: If all elements are guaranteed non-negative, use a two-pointer window: expand right, and while the running sum exceeds the target, shrink from the left.
If negative numbers are possible, instead track a single running prefix value (sum or XOR) and record the first index at which each prefix value was seen, in a hashmap.

At each index, check whether (running prefix minus target) for sums, or (running prefix XOR target) for XOR, was seen before; if so, the subarray between that earlier index and the current one matches the target.
When searching for the longest match, only store the first occurrence of each prefix value — never overwrite it — so the earliest possible left boundary is always preferred.

variations.name: Subarray with Given Sum (Non-Negative Elements)
variations.summary: Two-pointer window expands and shrinks monotonically to find a contiguous run summing exactly to the target
variations.inputShape: Single array of non-negative integers, one target sum
variations.movement: Two pointers — right expands, left shrinks while the running sum exceeds the target
variations.recognizeIt: 'Find a subarray that sums to exactly S' with an explicit guarantee that all elements are non-negative — that guarantee is what makes a simple growing/shrinking window valid instead of needing a hashmap.
variations.keyInsight: Because every element is non-negative, the running sum only ever increases as the window grows, so shrinking from the left whenever the sum overshoots the target is always the correct move — it can never skip past the answer.

variations.name: Longest Subarray with Sum Equal to K (Handles Negatives)
variations.summary: Prefix-sum hashmap storing the first-seen index per prefix value, used to find the longest matching subarray rather than just counting matches
variations.inputShape: Single array that may include negatives, one target k
variations.movement: Single forward pass, hashmap keyed by prefix sum → earliest index
variations.recognizeIt: 'Longest subarray summing to k' on an array that may contain negatives — negatives break the sliding window, and asking for the 'longest' (rather than a 'count') is why only the first occurrence of each prefix sum is kept.
variations.keyInsight: Storing only the first index each prefix sum was ever seen at (never overwriting it) guarantees the earliest possible left boundary is used whenever a match is found later, which is what maximizes the resulting subarray's length.

variations.name: Longest Subarray with XOR Equal to K
variations.summary: The same prefix-hashmap idea as the sum version above, with XOR replacing subtraction
variations.movement: Single forward pass, hashmap keyed by prefix XOR → earliest index
variations.recognizeIt: 'Longest subarray whose XOR equals k' — structurally the XOR twin of the negative-number-safe sum variation, with the same first-occurrence-only hashmap rule.
variations.keyInsight: prefixXor[i] ^ k gives exactly the earlier prefix value that, when XOR-ed again with prefixXor[i], cancels the shared portion down to precisely k across that span.

patterns.name: On Index
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: Binary search on index repeatedly halves a sorted search space to locate a target value or a boundary position in O(log n) instead of scanning linearly. At each step you compare the middle element to the target and discard the half that cannot contain the answer. Beyond finding an exact match, the same halving logic locates the first or last position where a condition becomes true, or — with an extra check per step — finds a target even when the sorted array has been rotated at an unknown pivot.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Finding whether a target value exists in a sorted array
Finding the first or last index at which a target appears
Finding a target in a sorted array that has been rotated at an unknown point
Reducing an O(n) linear scan to O(log n) whenever the search space is sorted or otherwise monotonic
Locating an insertion point that keeps an array sorted

steps: Set low = 0 and high = len(arr) - 1.
While low <= high, compute mid = (low + high) // 2.
If arr[mid] equals the target, return mid.
If arr[mid] is less than the target, discard the left half by setting low = mid + 1; otherwise discard the right half by setting high = mid - 1.
If the loop ends without a match, the target is not present — return -1 (or the appropriate boundary, depending on the variation).

variations.name: Classic Binary Search
variations.summary: Find the index of a target in a sorted array with no duplicates to worry about
variations.movement: Halve the search space each step
variations.recognizeIt: 'Given a sorted array, find the index of target' with no mention of duplicates, rotation, or a range to return — the plainest possible setup.

variations.name: First and Last Occurrence (Lower/Upper Bound)
variations.summary: Two separate binary searches — one biased to keep searching left after a match, one biased to keep searching right — to find a target's boundary positions
variations.inputShape: Sorted array with possible duplicates, single target
variations.movement: Two halving passes, each continuing past a match instead of stopping
variations.recognizeIt: 'Find the first and last position of target' or 'count occurrences of target' in a sorted array with duplicates — the duplicates are what rule out the classic single-answer search.
variations.keyInsight: Instead of returning immediately on a match, keep narrowing toward one side (left for the first occurrence, right for the last) — the match is recorded but the search doesn't stop there.

variations.name: Search in Rotated Sorted Array
variations.summary: Standard halving, but at each step first determine which half is still properly sorted, then check if the target lies within that sorted half
variations.inputShape: Sorted array rotated at an unknown pivot, single target, no duplicates
variations.movement: Halve the search space, choosing direction based on which half is sorted
variations.recognizeIt: 'Sorted array that has been rotated at an unknown pivot' — the rotation is the explicit signal that a plain comparison against arr[mid] alone isn't enough.
variations.keyInsight: At least one half around mid is always properly sorted even after rotation; checking whether the target falls within that sorted half's range tells you which side to keep, without ever needing to locate the pivot itself.

patterns.name: On Answer
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: Binary search on answer applies the same halving idea to a range of *candidate answers* rather than to array indices. It requires a monotonic feasibility check — a function that, for any candidate value, can say 'this works' or 'this doesn't' such that all values on one side of the true answer fail and all values on the other side succeed. You binary search over that answer range, calling the feasibility check at each midpoint, and narrow toward the boundary between failing and succeeding candidates. This turns problems like 'minimize the maximum X' or 'maximize the minimum Y' into a search problem even though nothing about the input array itself is sorted.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Minimizing the maximum value of some derived quantity subject to a constraint (e.g. minimum shipping capacity to finish within D days)
Maximizing the minimum distance or gap subject to a placement constraint (e.g. placing cows as far apart as possible)
Finding the smallest rate or speed that still satisfies a deadline (e.g. slowest eating speed that finishes in time)
Any 'find the smallest/largest value such that condition(value) holds' problem where condition is monotonic in value, even when the underlying array is unsorted

steps: Identify the range of possible answers and set low/high to its minimum and maximum plausible values.
Write a feasible(candidate) function that returns True/False for whether that candidate value satisfies the problem's constraint — this function must be monotonic across the answer range.
Binary search over [low, high]: at each mid, call feasible(mid).

If feasible(mid) is True, record mid as a possible answer and narrow toward the better direction (usually high = mid - 1 when minimizing, or low = mid + 1 when maximizing); otherwise move to the other side.
Return the best recorded feasible candidate once low and high cross.

variations.name: Minimize the Maximum (Capacity/Weight Style)
variations.summary: Binary search the smallest capacity value for which a greedy simulation still finishes within the allowed number of groups/days
variations.inputShape: Array of weights/quantities plus a fixed number of allowed groups (e.g. days)
variations.movement: Search over capacity values; feasibility check greedily simulates loading and counts groups needed
variations.recognizeIt: 'Find the minimum capacity/weight/speed so that everything is done within D days/trips' — a fixed count of allowed groups is the constraint, and capacity is the thing being minimized.
variations.keyInsight: The feasibility check is monotonic: if some capacity C works within the day limit, any capacity greater than C also works, which is exactly what makes binary searching over capacity valid.

variations.name: Maximize the Minimum (Placement/Distance Style)
variations.summary: Binary search the largest minimum-distance value for which all items can still be placed at or beyond that spacing
variations.inputShape: Sorted array of positions plus a count of items to place
variations.movement: Search over distance values; feasibility check greedily places items and checks the count fits
variations.timeComplexity: O(n log n) for the sort, O(n log(range)) for the search
variations.recognizeIt: 'Place k items so that the minimum distance between any two is as large as possible' — maximizing a minimum gap, the mirror image of the minimize-the-maximum variation above.
variations.keyInsight: Feasibility flips the other direction here: if a minimum distance D is achievable, every smaller distance is also achievable, so the search tightens toward the largest D that still works rather than the smallest.

variations.name: Minimize the Maximum Rate/Speed
variations.summary: Binary search the slowest feasible rate for which a ceiling-division-based time simulation still meets a deadline
variations.inputShape: Array of pile sizes/quantities plus a time limit
variations.movement: Search over rate values; feasibility check sums ceiling-divided hours per pile against the limit
variations.recognizeIt: 'Find the minimum eating speed/rate so that everything finishes within H hours', where each pile takes ceil(size / rate) time units — the ceiling-division time cost per item is the tell that separates this from the capacity-style variation above.
variations.keyInsight: A faster speed can only ever reduce or keep equal the total hours needed, so the feasible-speeds region is a contiguous upper range — binary search converges on its lower boundary.

patterns.name: Variable Size Sliding Window
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: A variable-size sliding window keeps two pointers, left and right, marking the boundaries of a substring under consideration. The right pointer expands the window one character at a time, folding new characters into a running frequency map or counter. Whenever the window stops satisfying some validity condition, the left pointer advances, removing characters from the window state, until the condition is restored. Because both pointers only ever move forward across the string, the total work is linear instead of the quadratic cost of checking every substring individually.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Finding the longest substring that satisfies a uniqueness or count constraint
Finding the smallest substring that contains a required set of characters
Detecting whether any window matches a target multiset of characters
Counting substrings that meet a frequency-based condition
Avoiding a full re-scan of the string on every step

steps: Initialize left = 0 and an empty frequency map / window state
Iterate right from 0 to len(s) - 1, adding s[right] into the window state
While the window violates the problem's validity condition, remove s[left] from the window state and advance left
Once the window is valid (or at the boundary condition the problem cares about), update the running answer using [left, right]
Continue until right reaches the end of the string; return the tracked answer
stepByStepAlgorithm.code:
from collections import defaultdict

def variable_size_window(s, is_valid):
    """
    Generic variable-size sliding window skeleton.
    is_valid(window, left, right) -> bool: whether the current window
    satisfies the problem's condition.
    """
    window = defaultdict(int)
    left = 0
    best = 0
    for right in range(len(s)):
        window[s[right]] += 1

        while not is_valid(window, left, right):
            window[s[left]] -= 1
            if window[s[left]] == 0:
                del window[s[left]]
            left += 1

        best = max(best, right - left + 1)
    return best

variations.name: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
variations.summary: Expand while all characters in the window are unique; jump left the moment a duplicate re-enters.
variations.movement: Right expands every step; left jumps forward only when a duplicate character is seen
variations.recognizeIt: Phrasing like 'without repeating characters' or 'no character appears more than once' is the signal. The core decision rule: track the last-seen index of each character, and whenever it reappears inside the current window, jump left to just past its previous position.
variations.keyInsight: Instead of shrinking one character at a time, jump left directly to (last_seen_index_of_duplicate + 1), skipping unnecessary iterations.

variations.name: Minimum Window Substring
variations.summary: Expand until the window covers every required character (with multiplicity), then shrink to find the smallest such window.
variations.inputShape: Two strings: a source string and a target character multiset
variations.movement: Right expands until the window is 'complete'; left then shrinks as far as possible while the window stays complete
variations.recognizeIt: Look for 'smallest/minimum window/substring that contains all characters of...'. The decision rule: maintain a count of how many required characters are currently fully satisfied, and only shrink left while that count stays at the required total.
variations.keyInsight: A single 'missing' counter (rather than comparing two full frequency maps every step) turns the completeness check into O(1) instead of O(charset) per step.

variations.name: Anagram / Permutation in String
variations.summary: Slide a fixed-size window equal to the pattern's length and compare character-count signatures.
variations.inputShape: Two strings: a source string and a fixed-length pattern
variations.movement: Both left and right advance together, keeping window size constant at len(pattern)
variations.recognizeIt: Phrasing like 'permutation of', 'anagram of', or 'same character counts' with a fixed target length is the signal. The decision rule: maintain a running count difference between the window and the pattern, and check for a zero-difference match as the window slides.
variations.keyInsight: Because the window size is fixed, you never need an inner shrink loop — every step removes exactly one character while adding exactly one, keeping the comparison O(1) amortized with a match-count trick.

patterns.name: Two Pointers / Read-Write Pointers on Strings
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: This pattern uses two index variables moving through a string instead of nested loops or extra buffers. In the converging form, one pointer starts at each end and moves inward, comparing or swapping characters until they meet. In the read/write form, both pointers start at the same end, but the write pointer only advances when it needs to commit a result, letting the read pointer scan ahead freely. Either way, the string is processed in a single linear pass with O(1) extra pointer bookkeeping.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Checking whether a string reads the same forwards and backwards
Reversing a string or the words within it in place
Compressing runs of repeated characters into a shorter representation
Removing or rewriting characters in place without extra storage
Comparing characters from opposite ends under a relaxation rule (e.g. skip non-alphanumeric)

steps: Decide the pointer setup: converging (left = 0, right = len(s) - 1) or read/write (read = 0, write = 0)
Loop while the pointers haven't crossed or the read pointer hasn't exhausted the string
On each iteration, inspect/compare the character(s) at the current pointer position(s)
Apply the problem-specific action: swap, skip, write, or count
Advance the pointer(s) according to the movement rule (inward for converging, forward for read/write)
Return the final result once the loop terminates
stepByStepAlgorithm.code:
def converging_two_pointer(s, matches):
    """
    Generic converging two-pointer skeleton.
    matches(a, b) -> bool: whether characters a and b satisfy the
    problem's comparison rule.
    """
    left, right = 0, len(s) - 1
    while left < right:
        if not matches(s[left], s[right]):
            return False
        left += 1
        right -= 1
    return True

variations.name: Palindrome Check
variations.summary: Converge from both ends, comparing characters (optionally skipping non-relevant ones).
variations.movement: Left and right start at opposite ends and move inward together
variations.recognizeIt: Any 'is this a palindrome' framing, including variants that ignore case/punctuation or allow deleting one character. The decision rule: if the two ends ever disagree under the comparison rule, it isn't a (simple) palindrome.
variations.keyInsight: The 'skip non-alphanumeric' inner while-loops keep the algorithm O(1) space and O(n) time even with filtering, because each character is still only inspected a constant number of times overall.

variations.name: Reverse Words / Characters
variations.summary: Converging pointers swap characters in place; a variant reverses word order using split/join or a two-pass reverse.
variations.inputShape: Single string (or list of characters for in-place reversal)
variations.movement: Left and right swap and move inward for character reversal; word reversal uses a reverse-then-reverse-each-word trick
variations.spaceComplexity: O(1) for character reversal in place; O(n) if strings are immutable and a new result must be built
variations.recognizeIt: 'Reverse the string/characters in place' signals the simple converging swap. 'Reverse the order of words' signals the two-level reversal (reverse whole string, then reverse each word back).
variations.keyInsight: Reversing word order without extra space (in languages with mutable char arrays) is done by reversing the whole buffer once, then re-reversing each word segment in place — two linear passes, still O(n) total.

variations.name: String Compression
variations.summary: A read pointer scans runs of identical characters while a write pointer commits the compressed character + count.
variations.inputShape: Single string (often as a mutable character array)
variations.movement: Read pointer scans forward through a run; write pointer advances only when a compressed run is committed
variations.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra if compressing into the same array; O(n) if building a new string
variations.recognizeIt: 'Compress consecutive repeated characters into character+count' is the signal. The decision rule: count the length of the current run before writing anything, so the write pointer only ever moves forward, never overwriting unread data.
variations.code:
def compress(chars):
    write = 0
    read = 0
    n = len(chars)
    while read < n:
        char = chars[read]
        count = 0
        while read < n and chars[read] == char:
            read += 1
            count += 1
        chars[write] = char
        write += 1
        if count > 1:
            for digit in str(count):
                chars[write] = digit
                write += 1
    return write
variations.keyInsight: The read pointer must always finish counting a full run before the write pointer touches the array, otherwise it risks overwriting characters the read pointer still needs to see.

patterns.name: KMP (Knuth-Morris-Pratt) Failure Function
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: KMP searches for a pattern inside a text without ever backing up the text pointer. It precomputes an LPS (Longest Proper Prefix which is also Suffix) array for the pattern itself, which encodes, for every prefix of the pattern, how much of a match can be reused if a mismatch occurs. When a mismatch happens during the search, instead of restarting the pattern pointer from zero, KMP jumps it back using the LPS array, effectively skipping over comparisons that are guaranteed to fail. This keeps both the preprocessing and the search phase linear in the length of the pattern and text respectively.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Finding all occurrences of a pattern inside a text in linear time
Finding the first occurrence of a pattern efficiently
Detecting periodicity or repeated structure within a string
Avoiding the O(n*m) worst case of naive substring search
Serving as a building block for more advanced string algorithms

steps: Build the LPS array for the pattern: lps[i] = length of the longest proper prefix of pattern[0..i] that is also a suffix of it
Initialize two pointers: i for the text, j for the pattern, both starting at 0
Compare text[i] with pattern[j]; if they match, advance both i and j
If they mismatch and j > 0, set j = lps[j - 1] instead of resetting to 0, and retry the comparison at the same i
If they mismatch and j == 0, simply advance i
Whenever j equals the pattern length, a match ending at i - 1 has been found; record it and set j = lps[j - 1] to continue searching for further matches

variations.name: Build the LPS / Failure Array
variations.summary: Compute, for every prefix of the pattern, the length of its longest proper prefix that's also a suffix.
variations.movement: A single pass over the pattern with an internal 'length' pointer that only ever moves forward or falls back via lps[length-1]
variations.timeComplexity: O(m) where m is the pattern length
variations.recognizeIt: Any task asking for 'the failure function', 'prefix function', or 'longest prefix-suffix array' by itself, independent of a search. The decision rule: when characters match, extend the current prefix-suffix length; on mismatch, fall back using the array's own previous values rather than scanning from scratch.
variations.keyInsight: The fallback step length = lps[length - 1] is what makes the whole construction O(m) rather than O(m^2) — it reuses previously computed prefix-suffix information instead of recomputing it.

variations.name: Pattern Search Using KMP
variations.summary: Use the precomputed LPS array to find the first (or every) occurrence of a pattern in a text in linear time.
variations.movement: Text pointer i only ever moves forward; pattern pointer j advances on match and falls back via lps on mismatch
variations.recognizeIt: 'Find the first/all occurrences of a pattern in a string, efficiently' with a hint that naive search is too slow is the signal. The decision rule: on mismatch, never move the text pointer backward — only fall back the pattern pointer using the LPS array.
variations.keyInsight: The text pointer i never decreases, which is the entire source of KMP's linear-time guarantee — every character of the text is 'consumed' at most a constant number of times.

variations.name: Counting Occurrences with KMP
variations.summary: Run the KMP search to completion without stopping at the first match, tallying every match end position.
variations.movement: Same as pattern search, but continues past the first match by falling back j = lps[j-1] and keeps scanning
variations.recognizeIt: 'How many times does the pattern occur' (as opposed to 'find the first occurrence') is the signal. The decision rule: don't return early on the first match — record it, fall back j, and keep the text pointer moving.
variations.code:
def count_occurrences(text, pattern):
    return len(kmp_search(text, pattern))
variations.keyInsight: Overlapping occurrences are still found correctly because falling back to j = lps[j-1] (rather than j = 0) preserves any partial match that could seed the next occurrence.

patterns.name: Rabin-Karp (Rolling Hash)
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: Rabin-Karp searches for a pattern by comparing hash values instead of raw characters. It computes a hash of the pattern and a hash of the first window of the text, then slides the window across the text one character at a time, updating the hash in O(1) using a 'rolling' formula that removes the outgoing character's contribution and adds the incoming character's. Whenever the rolling hash of the current window matches the pattern's hash, the characters are compared directly to rule out hash collisions before declaring a true match.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Searching for a pattern in a text using hash comparisons instead of character comparisons
Finding all repeated substrings of a fixed length
Searching for multiple patterns of the same length in a single pass
Detecting near-duplicate or repeated fixed-length sequences (e.g. DNA k-mers)
Providing the hashing backbone for binary-search-on-length substring problems

steps: Choose a base and a large prime modulus for the polynomial hash
Compute the hash of the pattern and the hash of the first window (same length) of the text
Compare the pattern hash to the current window hash; if equal, verify with a direct character comparison to guard against collisions
Slide the window forward by one: remove the outgoing character's contribution and add the incoming character's, using the rolling hash formula
Repeat the compare-then-slide step until the window reaches the end of the text
Return all positions where a verified match occurred

variations.name: Single Pattern Search via Rolling Hash
variations.summary: Slide a fixed-size window and compare rolling hashes to locate one pattern's occurrences in a text.
variations.movement: Window slides by one character each step; hash is updated in O(1) instead of recomputed
variations.timeComplexity: O(n + m) average, O(n*m) worst case with many hash collisions
variations.recognizeIt: A generic 'find the pattern in the text' task where the interviewer hints at hashing rather than KMP. The decision rule: two hashes matching is only a candidate — always verify with a direct substring comparison.
variations.keyInsight: Skipping the verification step is a common bug source — without it, hash collisions silently produce false positive matches.

variations.name: Multiple / Repeated Pattern Search
variations.summary: Hash every fixed-length window of a string and use a set/map to detect repeats or a batch of target patterns at once.
variations.inputShape: Single long string (looking for repeated fixed-length substrings) or text + multiple same-length patterns
variations.movement: One rolling pass over the string, storing/looking up each window's hash in a set or hash map
variations.timeComplexity: O(n) average for a single string scan
variations.spaceComplexity: O(n) for the set of seen hashes
variations.recognizeIt: 'Find all repeated substrings of length k' or 'find any of these patterns in one pass' signals this variation. The decision rule: instead of one target hash, maintain a collection of hashes to compare each rolling window against.
variations.keyInsight: Storing the actual substring alongside the hash (not just the hash) is what makes this collision-safe, since a set of raw hashes alone can't distinguish a true repeat from a coincidental collision.

variations.name: Anagram Substring Search via Hashing
variations.summary: Use a sum/XOR-style hash of character counts (rather than a positional polynomial hash) so any character permutation produces the same hash.
variations.movement: Window slides by one; the count-based hash is updated by removing the outgoing character's count contribution and adding the incoming one's
variations.recognizeIt: Looks similar to the sliding-window anagram variation, but framed around hashing character frequency signatures rather than directly comparing Counter objects. The decision rule: because anagrams share the same character multiset regardless of order, a frequency-based (order-independent) hash naturally handles them.
variations.keyInsight: Because this hash is order-independent by construction (it's really a frequency count, not a positional polynomial), it directly detects anagrams without needing any position-sensitive rolling formula.

patterns.name: Z Algorithm
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: The Z algorithm builds a single array, the Z-array, where Z[i] is the length of the longest substring starting at position i that matches a prefix of the whole string. It computes this in linear time by maintaining a window [l, r] representing the rightmost prefix-matching segment found so far. When the current index falls inside that window, previously computed Z-values are reused to seed a lower bound instead of comparing from scratch, and only the remainder is checked character by character. This single array turns out to encode a surprising amount of structural information: it can locate a pattern inside a text, reveal a string's periods, and detect borders — all from one linear preprocessing pass.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Finding all occurrences of a pattern inside a text in linear time
Detecting the periods of a string (how it can be built from repeating a shorter prefix)
Finding the longest substring that is simultaneously a prefix, a suffix, and occurs somewhere inside the string
Computing borders (prefix-suffix matches) of a string efficiently
Serving as an alternative to KMP for substring search via a single concatenated Z-array

steps: Initialize the Z-array with Z[0] left as 0 (by convention, since matching the whole prefix against itself isn't meaningful), and window bounds l = 0, r = 0
For each index i from 1 to n-1: if i is inside the current window [l, r], seed Z[i] with min(r - i, Z[i - l]) instead of starting from zero
Extend Z[i] by directly comparing characters at position Z[i] and i + Z[i] as long as they match
If the newly extended match pushes past r (i.e. i + Z[i] > r), update the window to l = i, r = i + Z[i]
Repeat until every index has a computed Z-value; return the completed array

variations.name: Build the Z-Array
variations.summary: Compute, for every index, the length of the longest prefix-matching substring starting there.
variations.movement: A single left-to-right pass, with an internal window [l, r] that only ever expands rightward
variations.recognizeIt: Any task asking directly for 'the Z-array' or 'Z-function' of a string, independent of a downstream search. The decision rule: when the current index is inside the tracked window, reuse Z[i - l] as a starting point instead of comparing from position 0 again.
variations.keyInsight: The window [l, r] is what guarantees linear time — without it, every index would need its own O(n) comparison from scratch, giving O(n^2) instead of O(n).

variations.name: Pattern Search via Concatenation
variations.summary: Concatenate pattern + separator + text, compute the Z-array once, and read off matches wherever Z equals the pattern's length.
variations.movement: Same single-pass Z-array computation, applied to the concatenated string
variations.recognizeIt: 'Find all occurrences of a pattern in a text' where the interviewer wants a Z-array-based alternative to KMP. The decision rule: build combined = pattern + separator + text (separator must not appear in either), then any index with Z[i] == len(pattern) marks a match.
variations.keyInsight: The separator character must be chosen so it never occurs in either string, otherwise a Z-value could 'leak' across the boundary and produce a false match.

variations.name: Finding Periods and Internal Borders
variations.summary: Use the Z-array of the string itself (no concatenation) to find repeating periods, or to find a border that also occurs strictly inside the string.
variations.movement: One Z-array pass, followed by a linear scan checking Z[i] + i == n (periods) or a running max scan for internal occurrences of a border
variations.recognizeIt: 'Find all periods of this string' or 'find the longest string that is a prefix, a suffix, and occurs in the middle' are the signals. The decision rule: L is a period exactly when Z[L] + L == n; a border of length L occurs internally when some Z[i] >= L for an i strictly between 0 and n-L.
variations.keyInsight: The check z[n - L] == L confirms the suffix of length L equals the prefix exactly (a border); a separate running maximum over interior Z-values confirms that same substring also occurs somewhere that isn't the prefix or suffix position itself.

patterns.name: Monotonic Stack
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: A monotonic stack keeps its elements in strictly increasing or strictly decreasing order at all times. Before pushing a new element, you pop off everything at the top that would break that order, and each popped element gets 'resolved' against the new element (its next greater/smaller value is now known). Because every element is pushed once and popped at most once, the whole scan runs in O(n) even though there's a while-loop inside a for-loop. It's the workhorse behind 'next/previous greater or smaller element' style problems.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Finding the next or previous greater/smaller element for every array position in O(n)
Building a smallest/largest possible number by deleting digits
Removing characters to get the lexicographically smallest valid string
Computing spans, ranges, or windows bounded by the nearest larger/smaller neighbor
Any 'for each element, look left/right until a bigger/smaller one shows up' problem

steps: Initialize an empty stack (holding indices) and a result array, typically filled with a sentinel like -1.
Decide the monotonic direction: increasing stack (pop while top >= current) finds 'next/previous smaller'; decreasing stack (pop while top <= current) finds 'next/previous greater'.
Scan the array once, left to right (or right to left for a 'previous' variant).
At each index i, while the stack is non-empty and its top index violates the chosen monotonic order relative to arr[i], pop it and record the answer for that popped index using i.
Push index i onto the stack so future elements can resolve against it.
After the scan, any indices still left on the stack have no qualifying neighbor and keep the sentinel value.
stepByStepAlgorithm.code:
def monotonic_stack(arr, find_greater=True, direction='next'):
    """
    Generic monotonic-stack scan.
    find_greater=True  -> decreasing stack, resolves the *next/previous greater* element
    find_greater=False -> increasing stack, resolves the *next/previous smaller* element
    direction='next'     -> scan left to right, result[i] gets filled when i is popped
    direction='previous' -> scan right to left, same popping rule, but records neighbors to the left
    Returns result[i] = index of the qualifying neighbor, or -1 if none exists.
    """
    n = len(arr)
    result = [-1] * n
    stack = []  # holds indices, kept monotonic per find_greater

    order = range(n) if direction == 'next' else range(n - 1, -1, -1)
    for i in order:
        while stack:
            top = stack[-1]
            violates = (arr[top] <= arr[i]) if find_greater else (arr[top] >= arr[i])
            if violates:
                result[top] = i
                stack.pop()
            else:
                break
        stack.append(i)

    return result

variations.name: Increasing Monotonic Stack
variations.summary: Stack values increase bottom-to-top; popping happens when the current element is smaller, so it resolves 'next/previous smaller element' problems.
variations.inputShape: Any array/string scanned once left to right
variations.movement: Single pass, left → right, pop-on-smaller
variations.recognizeIt: The problem talks about deleting/removing elements to make the result 'smallest possible' or asks for the next/previous *smaller* value. The decision rule: pop the stack while its top is >= the current element, because that top can never be the answer for anything smaller that still needs it.
variations.keyInsight: Each index is pushed once and popped at most once, so the amortized cost of the inner while-loop across the whole scan is O(n), not O(n^2).

variations.name: Decreasing Monotonic Stack
