variations.summary: Stack values decrease bottom-to-top; popping happens when the current element is larger, so it resolves 'next/previous greater element' problems.
variations.inputShape: Any array scanned once left to right
variations.movement: Single pass, left → right, pop-on-greater
variations.recognizeIt: The problem phrasing is 'next warmer day', 'next greater value', or 'first later element that's cheaper/bigger'. Decision rule: pop the stack while its top is < the current element, since the current element is now that top's answer.
variations.keyInsight: Because we only ever pop elements strictly smaller than the current one, equal values are left on the stack and correctly wait for a strictly greater neighbor, avoiding double-resolution bugs.

patterns.name: Nearest Element
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: This pattern is the applied form of the monotonic stack idea: for every position, find the nearest element to the left or right that's strictly greater or strictly smaller. A single left-to-right pass can answer all four combinations (next-greater, next-smaller, previous-greater, previous-smaller) by choosing when you record the answer: on pop for 'next' variants, or by reading the current stack top for 'previous' variants. It shows up constantly as a subroutine inside harder problems (histograms, subarray sums, spans).
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Finding, for every element, the nearest larger or smaller neighbor on a given side
Precomputing boundaries used later to size subarrays or windows
Detecting the first warmer/colder, cheaper/pricier, or taller/shorter neighbor
Feeding boundary indices into area, span, or contribution-sum calculations
Validating or simplifying sequences based on local peaks and valleys

steps: Initialize an empty stack of indices and a result array of sentinels (-1).
Pick the mode: which direction (next vs previous) and which comparator (greater vs smaller) the problem needs.
Scan the array once, left to right.
At each index i, pop indices from the stack whose values fail the comparator against arr[i].
If solving a 'next' variant, assign each popped index's answer to i as it's popped.
If solving a 'previous' variant, after popping finishes, the new top of the stack (if any) is i's answer.
Push i onto the stack and continue to the end of the array.

variations.name: Next Greater Element
variations.summary: For each index, find the nearest index to its right with a strictly larger value.
variations.movement: Left → right (or two passes for circular), pop-on-smaller-top
variations.recognizeIt: Phrases like 'next greater', 'next warmer day', or 'first bigger value to the right'. The decision rule: pop while the stack's top value is less than the current value — that top's answer is now known.
variations.keyInsight: For circular arrays, run the same scan over two virtual laps (index i % n) without pushing during the second lap, which lets wraparound answers resolve without extra bookkeeping.

variations.name: Next Smaller Element
variations.summary: For each index, find the nearest index to its right with a strictly (or equal) smaller value.
variations.movement: Left → right, pop-on-greater-top
variations.recognizeIt: Phrases like 'next smaller', 'first cheaper day', or 'apply a discount from the first later price that's no higher'. Decision rule: pop while the stack's top value is greater than (or equal to, depending on tie-handling needs) the current value.
variations.keyInsight: Whether you use > or >= as the pop condition decides how equal values are treated — >= treats equal values as 'smaller enough', which matters for problems like discount-pricing where a same-or-lower price still triggers the rule.

variations.name: Previous Greater / Previous Smaller
variations.summary: For each index, find the nearest index to its left with a strictly larger (or smaller) value, computed in one forward pass.
variations.movement: Left → right, but the answer is read from the stack top instead of assigned on pop
variations.recognizeIt: Phrases like 'previous greater', 'nearest smaller to the left', or boundary calculations needed before computing a span or subarray contribution. Decision rule: pop while the top violates the order, then whatever remains on top (if anything) is the answer — no separate reversed pass needed.
variations.code:
def previous_smaller_and_greater(nums):
    n = len(nums)
    prev_smaller = [-1] * n
    prev_greater = [-1] * n
    stack_s = []  # increasing stack, tracks previous smaller
    stack_g = []  # decreasing stack, tracks previous greater

    for i in range(n):
        while stack_s and nums[stack_s[-1]] >= nums[i]:
            stack_s.pop()
        prev_smaller[i] = stack_s[-1] if stack_s else -1
        stack_s.append(i)

        while stack_g and nums[stack_g[-1]] <= nums[i]:
            stack_g.pop()
        prev_greater[i] = stack_g[-1] if stack_g else -1
        stack_g.append(i)

    return prev_smaller, prev_greater
variations.keyInsight: Unlike the 'next' variants, 'previous' variants never need a reversed scan — the stack top *is* the previous qualifying neighbor by construction, right after the popping step finishes.

patterns.name: Range / Span
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: This pattern reuses previous-greater/previous-smaller (and next-greater/next-smaller) boundaries to measure a 'span' — how far you can extend left or right from a position before hitting something that breaks a condition. That span, multiplied by the element's own value, is the building block for stock-span problems and for contribution-technique sums like 'sum of subarray minimums' or 'sum of subarray ranges', where every element's contribution equals value × (positions to its left it dominates) × (positions to its right it dominates).
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Computing how many consecutive prior days a stock price has stayed at or below today's price
Summing the minimum (or maximum) value over every contiguous subarray efficiently
Summing max−min over every contiguous subarray without enumerating all of them
Any 'count/measure how far this element's influence reaches' problem
Turning an O(n²) or O(n³) brute force over subarrays into O(n)

steps: Initialize a decreasing stack of indices (by value) and a span array of 1s.
For each position i, pop all earlier indices whose value is <= the current value — they're within today's span.
If the stack empties out, the span reaches back to the start of the array; otherwise it's the gap to the nearest remaining strictly-greater index.
Record span[i] and push i onto the stack.
Repeat left to right for every position, giving each element's 'reach' in O(n) total.
Reuse the same left-pass and a mirrored right-pass to get both left- and right-reach for contribution-sum problems.
stepByStepAlgorithm.code:
def compute_span(prices):
    """
    Classic 'span': for each day, how many consecutive days (including
    today) have price <= today's price, walking back until a strictly
    greater price is hit.
    """
    n = len(prices)
    span = [1] * n
    stack = []  # decreasing stack of indices (by price)

    for i in range(n):
        while stack and prices[stack[-1]] <= prices[i]:
            stack.pop()
        span[i] = i - stack[-1] if stack else i + 1
        stack.append(i)

    return span

variations.name: Stock Span Problem
variations.summary: For each day's price, count how many consecutive prior days (including today) had a price less than or equal to today's.
variations.inputShape: Array or live stream of prices
variations.movement: Left → right, decreasing stack of (price, span)
variations.recognizeIt: The problem literally says 'span' or asks for consecutive days meeting a price condition, especially phrased as a streaming/online API. Decision rule: keep popping and accumulating span while the stack's stored price is <= the new price.
variations.keyInsight: Storing the span alongside the price in the stack (instead of just the index) lets a single pop absorb an entire run of previously-computed spans at once, keeping the streaming version O(1) amortized per call.

variations.name: Sum of Subarray Minimums (Contribution Technique)
variations.summary: Sum the minimum value across every contiguous subarray by computing, for each element, how many subarrays it is the minimum of.
variations.movement: Two passes: previous-strictly-smaller boundary, then next-smaller-or-equal boundary
variations.recognizeIt: The problem asks for a sum/count aggregated over *all* subarrays, which screams O(n²) brute force — the tell that you should instead ask 'how many subarrays is each element the min of?' Decision rule: for element i, left = distance to previous strictly-smaller element, right = distance to next smaller-or-equal element; contribution = value * left * right.
variations.code:
def sum_subarray_mins(arr, mod=10**9 + 7):
    n = len(arr)
    prev_less = [-1] * n     # previous strictly-smaller index
    next_less_eq = [n] * n   # next smaller-or-equal index
    stack = []

    for i in range(n):
        while stack and arr[stack[-1]] >= arr[i]:
            stack.pop()
        prev_less[i] = stack[-1] if stack else -1
        stack.append(i)

    stack = []
    for i in range(n - 1, -1, -1):
        while stack and arr[stack[-1]] > arr[i]:
            stack.pop()
        next_less_eq[i] = stack[-1] if stack else n
        stack.append(i)

    total = 0
    for i in range(n):
        left = i - prev_less[i]
        right = next_less_eq[i] - i
        total = (total + arr[i] * left * right) % mod
    return total
variations.keyInsight: Using a strict comparison on one side (previous) and a non-strict one on the other (next) is what prevents duplicate elements from being double-counted as each other's minimum-defining boundary.

variations.name: Sum of Subarray Maximums (Contribution Technique)
variations.summary: The mirror image of the minimum version: sum the maximum value across every contiguous subarray using previous/next greater boundaries.
variations.movement: Two passes: previous-strictly-greater boundary, then next-greater-or-equal boundary
variations.recognizeIt: Same shape as the minimum version but the problem wants max instead of min — or wants both, combined as (max − min) per subarray. Decision rule: flip every comparator from the minimum version (>= becomes <=, > becomes <).
variations.code:
def sum_subarray_ranges(arr):
    # sum of (max - min) over all subarrays = sum_of_max - sum_of_min
    def sum_subarray_extreme(arr, find_max):
        n = len(arr)
        prev = [-1] * n
        nxt = [n] * n
        stack = []
        pop_prev = (lambda a, b: a <= b) if find_max else (lambda a, b: a >= b)
        for i in range(n):
            while stack and pop_prev(arr[stack[-1]], arr[i]):
                stack.pop()
            prev[i] = stack[-1] if stack else -1
            stack.append(i)
        stack = []
        pop_next = (lambda a, b: a < b) if find_max else (lambda a, b: a > b)
        for i in range(n - 1, -1, -1):
            while stack and pop_next(arr[stack[-1]], arr[i]):
                stack.pop()
            nxt[i] = stack[-1] if stack else n
            stack.append(i)
        total = 0
        for i in range(n):
            left = i - prev[i]
            right = nxt[i] - i
            total += arr[i] * left * right
        return total

    return sum_subarray_extreme(arr, True) - sum_subarray_extreme(arr, False)
variations.keyInsight: Rather than writing a separate max-focused algorithm, the max and min contribution sums are structurally identical with comparators flipped — computing (sum of subarray max) − (sum of subarray min) directly answers 'sum of subarray ranges' without ever inspecting a subarray twice.

patterns.name: Min / Max Stack
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: A min/max stack augments a normal stack so it can report its current minimum (or maximum) in O(1) at any time, even as elements are pushed and popped in any order. The trick is to keep a second, parallel stack that always mirrors the 'running best' seen so far — every push records the min-so-far alongside the value, and every pop discards both together, so the running best is always instantly available and correctly rolls back when things are popped off.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Reporting the minimum or maximum of the current stack contents in O(1)
Supporting undo-style operations where the min/max must correctly 'roll back' as items are popped
Powering max-stack-based structures like max-stack with pop-max supporting arbitrary removal
Sliding-window minimum/maximum implemented via two such stacks (queue-via-two-stacks trick)
Any problem needing O(1) min/max queries interleaved with push/pop mutations

steps: Maintain two stacks: `main` for actual values, and `track` for the running minimum (or maximum) at each depth.
On push(x): push x onto `main`. Push onto `track` the smaller of x and track's current top (or just x if track is empty).
On pop(): pop from both `main` and `track` together, keeping them the same size at all times.
On getMin(): return track's top directly — no scanning needed.
On top(): return main's top directly.
Because both stacks shrink and grow in lockstep, popping the current minimum automatically 'reveals' the correct previous minimum.

variations.name: Min Stack
variations.summary: A stack that supports push, pop, top, and getMin, all in O(1).
variations.movement: Two stacks moving in lockstep
variations.recognizeIt: The problem explicitly asks to design a stack data structure with an O(1) minimum query. Decision rule: whenever you push, also push the smaller of (new value, current tracked min) onto a shadow stack.
variations.keyInsight: Storing the running min at every depth (rather than just once) trades O(n) extra space for guaranteeing correctness under arbitrary pop order — no recomputation ever needed.

variations.name: Max Stack
variations.summary: A stack supporting push, pop, top, peekMax, and popMax (removing the maximum element wherever it sits).
variations.movement: Two stacks in lockstep, plus a rebuild-on-popMax step
variations.timeComplexity: O(1) for push/pop/top/peekMax; O(n) for popMax
variations.recognizeIt: Same as min-stack but with an added twist: the problem wants to *remove* the max, not just read it, meaning elements above it must be preserved and re-pushed. Decision rule: pop everything above the max into a buffer, discard the max, then push the buffer back.
variations.keyInsight: popMax degrades to O(n) because removing an element from the middle of a stack fundamentally requires unwinding everything above it — a doubly linked list plus a sorted index (e.g. a balanced BST or skip list) is the standard trick to get all operations to O(log n).

patterns.name: Expression Handling
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: Expression handling uses one or two stacks to parse and evaluate arithmetic or logical expressions without recursion. One stack holds pending operands (numbers or partial results), and — for infix expressions — a second stack holds pending operators, popped and applied whenever precedence or a closing parenthesis demands it. This same operand/operator-stack machinery underlies calculators, expression validators, and conversions between infix, postfix, and prefix notation.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Evaluating a basic calculator expression with +, -, *, /, and parentheses
Evaluating expressions already in postfix (Reverse Polish) or prefix notation
Converting infix expressions to postfix or prefix form
Validating that parentheses/brackets in an expression are balanced and correctly nested
Decoding nested structures like nested strings or nested lists with brackets

steps: Initialize an operand stack (numbers) and, for infix parsing, an operator stack (symbols).
Scan the expression left to right, tokenizing numbers, operators, and parentheses as you go.
On a number, push it onto the operand stack.

On an operator, pop and apply operators from the operator stack while they have equal-or-higher precedence than the new one, pushing each result back onto the operand stack, then push the new operator.
On '(', push it onto the operator stack; on ')', pop and apply operators until the matching '(' is found and discarded.
After the scan, pop and apply any remaining operators; the operand stack's single remaining value is the answer.
stepByStepAlgorithm.code:
def evaluate_expression(expression):
    """
    Evaluates a basic calculator expression with +, -, *, /, and parentheses,
    using an operand stack and an operator stack with precedence handling.
    """
    def precedence(op):
        return 2 if op in ('*', '/') else 1

    def apply_op(a, b, op):
        if op == '+': return a + b
        if op == '-': return a - b
        if op == '*': return a * b
        if op == '/': return int(a / b)  # truncate toward zero

    operands = []
    operators = []
    i = 0
    n = len(expression)

    while i < n:
        ch = expression[i]
        if ch == ' ':
            i += 1
            continue
        elif ch.isdigit():
            num = 0
            while i < n and expression[i].isdigit():
                num = num * 10 + int(expression[i])
                i += 1
            operands.append(num)
            continue
        elif ch == '(':
            operators.append(ch)
        elif ch == ')':
            while operators[-1] != '(':
                b, a, op = operands.pop(), operands.pop(), operators.pop()
                operands.append(apply_op(a, b, op))
            operators.pop()  # discard '('
        else:  # operator
            while (operators and operators[-1] != '(' and
                   precedence(operators[-1]) >= precedence(ch)):
                b, a, top_op = operands.pop(), operands.pop(), operators.pop()
                operands.append(apply_op(a, b, top_op))
            operators.append(ch)
        i += 1

    while operators:
        b, a, op = operands.pop(), operands.pop(), operators.pop()
        operands.append(apply_op(a, b, op))

    return operands[-1]

variations.name: Basic Calculator (Infix Evaluation)
variations.summary: Evaluate an infix arithmetic expression string with +, -, *, /, and parentheses using a dual operand/operator stack.
variations.movement: Single left-to-right scan with two stacks
variations.recognizeIt: The problem gives a string like a calculator would accept, with operators, parentheses, and possibly spaces. Decision rule: pop-and-apply from the operator stack whenever the new operator's precedence is not higher than what's on top.
variations.keyInsight: Handling unary minus (e.g. '-(2+3)') requires pushing a 0 operand before the '-' whenever it appears at the start of the expression or right after '(' — an easy edge case to miss.

variations.name: Evaluate Reverse Polish Notation (Postfix)
variations.summary: Evaluate a postfix expression using a single operand stack — no operator stack or precedence rules needed.
variations.inputShape: Array or string tokens in postfix order
variations.movement: Single left-to-right scan, one stack
variations.recognizeIt: The tokens are already in postfix/RPN order (operators come after their operands, e.g. ['2','1','+','3','*']). Decision rule: on a number, push it; on an operator, pop two operands, apply, and push the result back.
variations.keyInsight: Postfix needs no precedence logic or parentheses handling at all — the notation itself has already encoded evaluation order, which is exactly why compilers convert to it internally.

variations.name: Balanced / Valid Parentheses
variations.summary: Check whether every opening bracket has a matching, correctly-nested closing bracket using a single stack.
variations.inputShape: String of bracket characters (possibly mixed with other characters)
variations.movement: Single left-to-right scan, one stack
variations.recognizeIt: The problem asks whether brackets/parentheses are 'balanced', 'valid', or 'well-formed'. Decision rule: push opening brackets; on a closing bracket, it must match the stack's top exactly, or the string is invalid.
variations.keyInsight: The two failure modes to check are symmetric but distinct: a closing bracket with nothing to match (stack empty), and a mismatched pair (stack top is the wrong opener) — both must be handled, and the stack must also be empty at the very end.

variations.name: Decode / Expand Nested Structure
variations.summary: Expand a compressed nested-bracket encoding (e.g. '3[a2[c]]' → 'accaccacc') using a stack to remember the state before each nesting level.
variations.inputShape: String with digits, letters, and brackets encoding repetition
variations.movement: Single left-to-right scan, stack of (previous-string, repeat-count) pairs
variations.recognizeIt: The string encodes repeated substrings with a number before a bracketed group, and groups can nest inside each other. Decision rule: on '[', push the current string-so-far and multiplier, then start fresh; on ']', pop and combine.
variations.keyInsight: Multi-digit repeat counts (e.g. '12[a]') must be accumulated digit by digit before a '[' is seen — a common off-by-one bug is resetting `num` too early or too late relative to the bracket.

patterns.name: Histogram Pattern
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: This pattern finds the largest rectangle that fits under a histogram-like sequence of bar heights, and its 2D generalization finds the largest all-1s or all-filled rectangle in a grid. Both rely on the same idea as Range/Span: for every bar, use previous-smaller and next-smaller boundaries (via a monotonic stack) to know exactly how far left and right that bar can extend before a shorter bar cuts it off, then compute area = height × width for every bar and take the max.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Finding the largest rectangular area under a histogram of bar heights
Finding the largest rectangle containing only 1s in a binary matrix
Finding the maximum square (not just rectangle) of 1s in a binary matrix
Any 'largest fitting shape under an uneven boundary' problem
Building-skyline or container-style area-maximization problems

steps: Initialize an empty stack of indices and a running max_area of 0. Conceptually append a height of 0 at the very end to force a final flush.
Scan the heights left to right. For each bar, while the stack is non-empty and the current bar is shorter than the bar at the stack's top, pop the top.
When popping index `top`, its height is height[top]; its width is (current index) - (new stack top index) - 1, or just the current index if the stack is now empty.
Update max_area with height[top] * width for that popped bar.
Push the current index onto the stack and continue.
After the scan (including the sentinel 0), max_area holds the answer.
For the 2D grid variant, treat each row as the 'ground' and accumulate column-wise heights (consecutive 1s upward) row by row, running the histogram algorithm on each resulting row.
stepByStepAlgorithm.code:
def largest_rectangle_area(heights):
    """
    Largest rectangle under a histogram, using a monotonic (increasing) stack
    of indices and previous/next-smaller boundaries computed on the fly.
    """
    stack = []          # indices, heights increasing bottom-to-top
    max_area = 0
    heights = heights + [0]  # sentinel to flush remaining bars

    for i, h in enumerate(heights):
        while stack and heights[stack[-1]] > h:
            height = heights[stack.pop()]
            width = i if not stack else i - stack[-1] - 1
            max_area = max(max_area, height * width)
        stack.append(i)

    return max_area

variations.name: Largest Rectangle in Histogram
variations.summary: Find the maximum-area rectangle that fits entirely under a sequence of histogram bar heights.
variations.movement: Single left-to-right pass with an increasing stack, plus a sentinel 0 at the end
variations.recognizeIt: The problem literally shows or describes a histogram/bar chart and asks for the largest rectangle fitting under it. Decision rule: whenever the current bar is shorter than the stack's top, that popped bar's rectangle is now fully determined — compute height × width immediately.
variations.code:
def largest_rectangle_area(heights):
    stack = []
    max_area = 0
    heights = heights + [0]
    for i, h in enumerate(heights):
        while stack and heights[stack[-1]] > h:
            height = heights[stack.pop()]
            width = i if not stack else i - stack[-1] - 1
            max_area = max(max_area, height * width)
        stack.append(i)
    return max_area
variations.keyInsight: Appending a sentinel bar of height 0 at the very end guarantees every remaining bar on the stack gets popped and evaluated, avoiding a separate cleanup loop after the main scan.

variations.name: Maximal Rectangle in Binary Matrix
variations.summary: Find the largest all-1s rectangle in a 2D binary matrix by running the histogram algorithm once per row, treating each row as ground level.
variations.movement: Row-by-row: accumulate column heights downward, then histogram-scan each row
variations.recognizeIt: The problem is the histogram problem but stated over a 2D grid of 0/1 instead of a 1D array of heights. Decision rule: for each row, height[col] = 0 if matrix[row][col] == '0', else height[col] + 1 from the row above — then reuse the histogram routine unchanged.
variations.keyInsight: Reusing the exact same O(n) histogram routine per row (rather than re-deriving a 2D algorithm) turns an apparently harder 2D problem into rows independent O(cols) calls, for a clean O(rows × cols) total.

patterns.name: FIFO Processing (Enqueue/Dequeue Order)
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: A queue processes elements strictly in the order they arrive: whatever goes in first comes out first. You push new items onto the back and remove items from the front, so the queue naturally models real-world lines, buffers, and streams where processing order matters. Almost every 'process things in the order they arrived' problem reduces to maintaining a FIFO container with O(1) push/pop at the correct ends. Python's collections.deque gives O(1) appends and pops from both ends, making it the natural backing structure even when only one end's FIFO behavior is actually used.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Simulating a real queue (customers, print jobs, requests) in arrival order
Implementing a queue using two stacks or a fixed array
Rate limiting / counting events within a trailing time window
Streaming aggregates like moving averages
Buffering producer/consumer data safely
Round-robin scheduling of tasks

steps: Initialize an empty queue (deque) to hold items in arrival order.
When a new item arrives, append it to the back of the queue.
When you need to process the oldest item, pop from the front of the queue.
If the problem needs a bounded window, pop from the front whenever old items fall outside the window (based on time/index/count).
Repeat until all items are processed or the input stream ends.

variations.name: Basic Queue Simulation
variations.summary: Plain enqueue/dequeue with no extra bookkeeping
variations.movement: back → front
variations.recognizeIt: The problem literally asks you to 'implement a queue' or process operations 'in the order received,' with no time window or priority involved — a direct enqueue/dequeue mapping.
variations.keyInsight: Two stacks give you a queue because reversing a reversed order restores the original order — pushing onto the out-stack flips the in-stack's LIFO order back into FIFO order.

variations.name: Moving Average from Data Stream
variations.summary: Maintain a running sum over a fixed-size trailing window
variations.movement: back → front (evict when window full)
variations.recognizeIt: Keywords 'moving average,' 'sliding window over a stream,' or 'last k values' — you need the average of a *fixed* recent window on every new insert.
variations.keyInsight: Track a running sum instead of re-summing the window each call — add the incoming value and subtract the evicted one.

variations.name: Number of Recent Calls (Trailing Time Window Count)
variations.summary: Count how many events fall within the last T milliseconds
variations.movement: back → front (evict expired timestamps)
variations.recognizeIt: 'How many requests/pings/events happened in the last X ms/seconds' with timestamps guaranteed non-decreasing — evict from the front whenever a timestamp falls outside the trailing window.

variations.name: Simulate Repeated Reveal/Requeue Process
variations.summary: Model a repeated 'take front, act, requeue next' real-world process
variations.inputShape: Array to be rearranged, plus an index queue
variations.movement: front → back (requeue) / front → out (reveal)
variations.recognizeIt: The problem describes a repeated physical action — 'reveal top card, then move next card to bottom' — which is a direct simulation of a FIFO index queue rather than a closed-form formula.
variations.code:
from collections import deque
from typing import List

def deckRevealedIncreasing(deck: List[int]) -> List[int]:
    n = len(deck)
    index_queue = deque(range(n))
    result = [0] * n
    for card in sorted(deck):
        result[index_queue.popleft()] = card
        if index_queue:
            index_queue.append(index_queue.popleft())
    return result
variations.keyInsight: Simulate the reveal process on indices 0..n-1 first (with a queue) to learn which original position gets which sorted rank, then place the sorted values into those positions.

patterns.name: Level-order / Level-wise Processing (BFS Layers)
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: Level-wise processing uses a queue to process a graph or tree one 'layer' at a time — everything at distance k from the start is fully handled before anything at distance k+1 is looked at. You do this by capturing the current queue's length at the start of each round, then popping exactly that many nodes before pushing any of their children, which keeps the layers cleanly separated. This is the backbone of breadth-first search whenever the problem cares about depth, distance, or level rather than just reachability.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Binary tree level order traversal
Shortest path in an unweighted graph
Finding the level with the maximum/minimum sum
Zigzag / boundary traversal of a tree
Multi-source BFS (rotting oranges, walls and gates)
Finding the rightmost/leftmost node per level

steps: Initialize a queue with the starting node(s) and a level counter at 0.
While the queue is not empty, record its current length — this is exactly how many nodes belong to the current level.
Pop that many nodes one at a time, processing each and pushing its unvisited neighbors/children onto the back of the queue.
Once that many pops are done, the current level is finished; increment the level counter.
Repeat until the queue is empty.
stepByStepAlgorithm.code:
from collections import deque

def bfs_by_level(start_nodes, get_neighbors):
    queue = deque(start_nodes)
    visited = set(start_nodes)
    level = 0
    levels_output = []
    while queue:
        level_size = len(queue)          # snapshot: nodes belonging to this level
        current_level = []
        for _ in range(level_size):
            node = queue.popleft()
            current_level.append(node)
            for neighbor in get_neighbors(node):
                if neighbor not in visited:
                    visited.add(neighbor)
                    queue.append(neighbor)
        levels_output.append(current_level)
        level += 1
    return levels_output

variations.name: Binary Tree Level Order Traversal
variations.summary: Group node values into one list per depth level
variations.movement: parent → children, layer by layer
variations.recognizeIt: 'Return node values grouped level by level' — you need each level as its own list, not a flat traversal.

variations.name: Zigzag Level Order Traversal
variations.summary: Alternate left-to-right and right-to-left per level
variations.movement: parent → children, direction flips per level
variations.recognizeIt: Same as level order but output direction alternates by level parity — reverse every other level's list.
variations.keyInsight: Reuse plain level-order BFS; only flip the direction of the output list on odd levels — the traversal/queue logic never changes.

variations.name: Shortest Path via BFS Level Count
variations.summary: BFS layer count equals shortest distance in an unweighted graph/grid
variations.movement: current cell/node → unvisited neighbors, layer by layer
variations.timeComplexity: O(V + E) or O(rows × cols) for grids
variations.recognizeIt: 'Shortest path/minimum steps' in an unweighted graph or grid — the level at which you first reach the target IS the answer.
variations.keyInsight: In an unweighted graph, BFS level number equals shortest distance from the source because BFS explores nodes in strictly increasing distance order.

variations.name: Multi-Source BFS
variations.summary: Start BFS from many sources simultaneously to spread outward in lockstep
variations.movement: all sources → neighbors simultaneously, layer by layer
variations.recognizeIt: 'Multiple starting points spreading simultaneously' (fire, rot, infection, gates) and you need the time until everything is reached/converted.
variations.keyInsight: Seed the queue with all sources before the first level starts, so the level counter measures simultaneous spread time rather than one source's individual BFS.

patterns.name: Circular Queue (Ring Buffer)
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: A circular queue stores elements in a fixed-size array and wraps the front/rear indices back to 0 using modulo arithmetic once they hit the end, instead of shifting elements or growing unboundedly. This reuses freed slots at the front of the array as soon as they're dequeued, giving true O(1) enqueue/dequeue in fixed memory — the classic 'ring buffer.' It also underlies problems where positions wrap around a fixed-size structure, like a circular array or a clock.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Implementing a fixed-capacity queue without reallocation (ring buffers)
Circular array traversal problems (next greater element II, gas station)
Buffering streaming data with a fixed memory budget
Round-robin resource allocation
Distinguishing full vs empty state without ambiguous index comparisons

steps: Allocate a fixed-size array of capacity k, plus front index, rear index, and a size counter (all starting at 0).
To enqueue: if size == k, reject/overwrite per spec; otherwise write at rear, then set rear = (rear + 1) % k and increment size.
To dequeue: if size == 0, reject; otherwise read at front, then set front = (front + 1) % k and decrement size.
Use the size counter (or a full/empty flag) rather than comparing front == rear, since that comparison alone is ambiguous between 'empty' and 'full.'
Repeat enqueue/dequeue operations, letting indices wrap around via modulo.
stepByStepAlgorithm.code:
class CircularQueue:
    def __init__(self, capacity):
        self.capacity = capacity
        self.buffer = [None] * capacity
        self.front = 0
        self.rear = 0
        self.size = 0

    def enqueue(self, value):
        if self.size == self.capacity:
            return False                       # queue is full
        self.buffer[self.rear] = value
        self.rear = (self.rear + 1) % self.capacity   # wrap around
        self.size += 1
        return True

    def dequeue(self):
        if self.size == 0:
            return None                        # queue is empty
        value = self.buffer[self.front]
        self.front = (self.front + 1) % self.capacity # wrap around
        self.size -= 1
        return value

variations.name: Design Circular Queue
variations.summary: Direct ring buffer implementation with fixed capacity k
variations.inputShape: Sequence of enqueue/dequeue/peek operation calls, fixed capacity k
variations.movement: rear write / front read, wrap via modulo
variations.recognizeIt: 'Design a circular queue with fixed capacity k' — directly implement the ring buffer with front/rear/size bookkeeping.
variations.keyInsight: Use a separate size counter instead of comparing front==rear to distinguish empty from full — that comparison is ambiguous on its own.

variations.name: Design Circular Deque
variations.summary: Same ring buffer, but insertion/removal allowed at both ends
variations.inputShape: Sequence of insertFront/insertLast/deleteFront/deleteLast calls, fixed capacity k
variations.movement: both ends wrap via modulo
variations.recognizeIt: 'Design a circular double-ended queue' — same buffer, but you also need front-insert and rear-remove, so front can decrement (mod capacity) too.
variations.keyInsight: Decrementing front wraps to capacity-1 using (front - 1 + capacity) % capacity — the + capacity guards against negative results before the modulo.

variations.name: Next Greater Element in a Circular Array
variations.summary: Traverse a circular array (wrap once) to find each element's next greater element
variations.movement: index wraps via modulo across two full passes
variations.recognizeIt: 'Next greater/smaller element, but the array is circular' — you conceptually loop the array twice (i % n) so elements near the end can see elements near the start.
variations.keyInsight: Iterate 2n times using i % n to simulate wraparound, combined with a monotonic stack — you don't need to physically duplicate the array.

variations.name: Circular Route Feasibility (Gas Station)
variations.summary: Find the valid circular starting point using a running-total wraparound argument
variations.inputShape: Two arrays (gas, cost) arranged in a circle
variations.movement: running total resets forward whenever it goes negative
variations.recognizeIt: 'Circular route / can you complete the circuit' phrasing with a resource that must never go negative — track a running total and reset the candidate start whenever it dips below zero.
variations.code:
from typing import List

def canCompleteCircuit(gas: List[int], cost: List[int]) -> int:
    total_surplus = 0
    running_total = 0
    start_index = 0
    for i in range(len(gas)):
        diff = gas[i] - cost[i]
        total_surplus += diff
        running_total += diff
        if running_total < 0:
            start_index = i + 1
            running_total = 0
    return start_index if total_surplus >= 0 else -1
variations.keyInsight: If the total resource across the whole circle is non-negative, exactly one valid start exists, and it's the index right after the running total's lowest point.

patterns.name: Deque-Based Sliding Window Optimization
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: A deque (double-ended queue) lets you push and pop from both ends in O(1), which is exactly what you need to maintain a monotonic sequence of candidates as a window slides across an array. Instead of storing every element in the window, you keep only the ones that could still matter — discarding from the back whenever a new, better element makes older ones useless, and discarding from the front once elements fall outside the window. This turns what looks like an O(n·k) sliding-window-maximum problem into O(n), since each element is pushed and popped at most once.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Sliding window maximum/minimum
Shortest subarray with a sum at least K
Maintaining a monotonic sequence while a window moves
Palindrome checking by comparing from both ends
Jump game reachability within a bounded window
Constrained subsequence sum with a bounded index gap

steps: Initialize an empty deque to store indices (not values) of candidate elements.
For each new index i, while the deque's back index has a value that is 'dominated' by nums[i] (per the problem's monotonic rule), pop it from the back.
Push index i onto the back of the deque.
While the deque's front index is outside the current window (i - front_index >= window_size), pop it from the front.
Once the window is fully formed, the deque's front index holds the answer (max/min) for the current window; record it and slide forward.

variations.name: Sliding Window Maximum
variations.summary: Monotonic decreasing deque of indices to track the max in each window
variations.movement: back pops dominated elements, front pops expired elements
variations.recognizeIt: 'Maximum of every window of size k' — the classic signal for a monotonic deque; a naive per-window scan would be O(n·k).
variations.keyInsight: Once a new element is larger than everything behind it in the deque, those smaller elements can never be the max again for any future window — safe to discard permanently.

variations.name: Shortest Subarray with Sum At Least K
variations.summary: Monotonic deque over prefix sums to find the shortest valid window
variations.inputShape: Array (possibly with negatives) + target sum K
variations.movement: back pops non-increasing prefix sums, front pops once a valid window is found
variations.recognizeIt: 'Shortest subarray with sum >= K' where the array can contain negative numbers — a plain two-pointer window fails, so you maintain a monotonic deque of prefix-sum indices instead.
variations.keyInsight: Only strictly increasing prefix sums can ever start a shortest valid window, so any prefix sum >= the newest one is permanently useless and gets popped from the back.

variations.name: Constrained Subsequence Sum (Bounded Gap DP + Deque)
variations.summary: Use a deque to fetch the best DP value within the last k indices in O(1)
variations.inputShape: Array + max allowed index gap k
variations.movement: back pops dominated DP values, front pops expired indices
variations.recognizeIt: A DP recurrence where dp[i] depends on max(dp[i-k..i-1]) — a sliding-window-maximum embedded inside a 1D DP, so keep a monotonic deque of DP values alongside the DP array.
variations.code:
from collections import deque
from typing import List

def constrainedSubsetSum(nums: List[int], k: int) -> int:
    n = len(nums)
    dp = [0] * n
    dq = deque()  # indices, dp-values in decreasing order
    best = nums[0]
    for i in range(n):
        if dq and dq[0] < i - k:
            dq.popleft()
        prev_best = dp[dq[0]] if dq else 0
        dp[i] = nums[i] + max(prev_best, 0)
        best = max(best, dp[i])
        while dq and dp[dq[-1]] <= dp[i]:
            dq.pop()
        dq.append(i)
    return best
variations.keyInsight: Treat max(dp[i-k..i-1]) as a sliding window maximum problem over the dp array itself, reusing the exact same monotonic-deque mechanics as Sliding Window Maximum.

variations.name: Two-Ended Comparison (Palindrome/Deque Checks)
variations.summary: Compare characters from both ends of a deque inward to verify symmetry
variations.movement: both ends → inward, one comparison at a time
variations.recognizeIt: 'Check whether a sequence is a palindrome/valid from both ends' where you need O(1) access and removal at both the front and back simultaneously — a natural fit for deque.popleft()/pop() in lockstep.
variations.keyInsight: A deque gives O(1) pop from both ends, so verifying symmetry costs O(n) total instead of repeatedly slicing/indexing a string.

patterns.name: Frequency Counting
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: Build a hash map that records how many times each element appears in a collection, in a single pass. Once the counts exist, most questions about the data (duplicates, majority values, rarest values, comparing two collections) become simple lookups or comparisons instead of nested loops. The map turns 'how many times does X occur' from an O(n) scan into an O(1) check.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Counting occurrences of each element
Finding the first non-repeating element
Checking if two strings are anagrams of each other
Finding a majority or most-frequent element
Comparing the contents of two collections
Ranking elements by how often they appear

steps: Initialize an empty hash map to store element -> count
Iterate through the input once, incrementing each element's count (defaulting missing keys to 0)
Once the map is built, iterate again (over the map or the original input) to answer the specific question
Use the counts to look up, compare, or find an extremal value as the final answer
Return the result once the condition is satisfied

variations.name: Basic Frequency Count
variations.summary: Count occurrences of each element/character in one pass and use the counts directly
variations.movement: Single forward pass, no pointers
variations.recognizeIt: Keywords like 'how many times', 'occurrences', 'first unique', or 'is an anagram of' signal this. The decision rule: if the answer only depends on how often things appear (not their order or position), a plain frequency map is enough.

variations.name: Majority Element via Counting
variations.summary: Track counts while scanning and return early the moment one element's count crosses n/2
variations.movement: Single forward pass
variations.recognizeIt: The phrase 'appears more than n/2 times' or 'majority element' signals this. Unlike Boyer-Moore voting, this trades O(1) space for a more intuitive, directly-verifiable count.
variations.keyInsight: Because a majority element by definition exceeds n/2 occurrences, you can return as soon as any single count crosses that threshold instead of finishing the full scan.

variations.name: Top K Frequent Elements
variations.summary: Build a frequency map, then sort or heap-select the k highest counts
variations.movement: One pass to count, then a sort/selection step
variations.timeComplexity: O(n log n) with sorting (O(n log k) with a heap)
variations.recognizeIt: 'Top k', 'k most frequent', or 'rank by frequency' are the signals. The decision rule: build the map first, then treat ranking as a separate, decoupled step.
variations.keyInsight: A min-heap of size k avoids sorting all n counts, improving to O(n log k) when k is much smaller than n.

patterns.name: Constant-Time Lookup
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: Store values you've already seen in a hash map as you scan, so that checking whether a needed value (or its complement) exists becomes an O(1) lookup instead of a nested-loop search. This turns classic O(n^2) brute-force pair/existence problems into a single O(n) pass.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Finding a pair of numbers that sum to a target
Checking whether a value has already been seen
Detecting the complement needed to satisfy a condition
Avoiding nested loops for existence checks
Caching (memoizing) computed results to avoid recomputation

steps: Initialize an empty hash map to record values seen so far, plus any needed metadata (like index)
Iterate through the input once
For each element, compute the complement or condition needed to satisfy the problem
Check if that complement already exists in the map; if so, resolve the answer immediately
Otherwise, insert the current element into the map and continue
Return the result once found, or a default value if the loop completes with no match

variations.name: Complement Sum Lookup
variations.summary: Store seen values while scanning; check whether target minus the current value already exists
variations.movement: Single forward pass
variations.recognizeIt: 'Pair that sums to a target' with unsorted input or a need for original indices signals this over a sorted two-pointer approach.
variations.keyInsight: Checking for the complement before inserting the current element prevents using the same element twice.

variations.name: Seen-Before Existence Check
variations.summary: Use a hash set (or map used as a set) to detect whether a value has appeared before, in O(1)
variations.movement: Single forward pass
variations.recognizeIt: 'Does any value repeat', 'contains duplicate', or 'has this been seen before' are the signals.

variations.name: Memoized Lookup (Caching)
variations.summary: Use a hash map as a cache so an expensive or recursive computation is never repeated for the same input
variations.movement: Recursive calls short-circuited by map lookups
variations.recognizeIt: Naive recursion that recomputes the same subproblem many times (like plain recursive Fibonacci) signals this pattern.
variations.keyInsight: The map's key is the subproblem's input, not a raw value from the data — the same lookup mechanism generalizes beyond arrays to any repeated computation.

patterns.name: Hash Set Membership
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: Uses a hash set — a hash map that only cares about keys — to track uniqueness or membership. This enables instant existence checks, deduplication, and set-algebra operations like intersection, without nested loops. It's especially powerful combined with a sliding window, where the set represents 'what's currently in the window.'
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Removing duplicates from a collection
Checking whether two collections share elements
Detecting a repeat/cycle via a 'visited' set
Enforcing a uniqueness constraint within a window
Finding elements missing or extra between two collections

steps: Initialize an empty hash set
Iterate through the input elements
For each element, check membership in the set in O(1)
Add the element to the set (or remove earlier elements if maintaining a shrinking window)
Use the final set, or the results collected along the way, to build the answer
stepByStepAlgorithm.code:
def longest_unique_substring(s):
    seen = set()
    left = 0
    best = 0
    for right, ch in enumerate(s):
        while ch in seen:
            seen.remove(s[left])
            left += 1
        seen.add(ch)
        best = max(best, right - left + 1)
    return best

variations.name: Deduplication / Intersection
variations.summary: Convert collections to sets and use set operations to find common or unique elements
variations.movement: No pointers — direct set-algebra
variations.recognizeIt: 'Intersection', 'union', or 'elements common to both' with no need to preserve duplicates or order.
variations.code:
def intersection(nums1, nums2):
    return sorted(set(nums1) & set(nums2))

variations.name: Sliding Window with a Visited Set
variations.summary: Maintain a set of 'currently in window' elements while sliding a window to keep everything inside it unique
variations.movement: Two pointers (left, right) with a set tracking the window's contents
variations.recognizeIt: 'Longest substring without repeating characters' or any 'longest/shortest window with no duplicates' phrasing.
variations.code:
def length_of_longest_substring(s):
    seen = set()
    left = 0
    best = 0
    for right, ch in enumerate(s):
        while ch in seen:
            seen.remove(s[left])
            left += 1
        seen.add(ch)
        best = max(best, right - left + 1)
    return best
variations.keyInsight: Shrinking the window from the left only until the duplicate is removed (rather than resetting entirely) keeps the algorithm linear.

variations.name: Visited-State Cycle Detection
variations.summary: Use a set to mark previously visited states in a sequence, so a repeated state signals a cycle
variations.inputShape: A sequence generated by repeatedly applying a transformation
variations.movement: Iterative transformation with a growing visited set
variations.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is states visited before a cycle or termination
variations.recognizeIt: 'Determine if this process loops forever' or 'will this reach 1' style problems, where re-encountering a prior state means it will never terminate.
variations.keyInsight: If a state repeats, the process is provably stuck in an infinite loop — you don't need to prove this mathematically, just detect the repeat.

patterns.name: Value-to-Index Mapping
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: Stores each value's position (or a derived key) as the map's value, so once a target relationship is found you can immediately recover where the matching elements are — not just whether they exist. This is the natural extension of lookup-based patterns when the answer needs positions, distances between positions, or ordering, rather than a plain true/false.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Returning indices of elements satisfying a condition
Mapping values to their most recent (or first) occurrence
Detecting duplicate values within a bounded index distance
Building an inverse index for O(1) position lookups
Counting pairs of equal values by their positions

steps: Initialize an empty hash map to store value -> index
Iterate through the array with both index and value
For each value, check the map for a related value or the value itself
If found, check the stored index against the current index and any given distance constraint
Update the map with the current value's index (overwriting if only the latest position matters)
Return the result once the constraint is satisfied, or a default value if the loop completes

variations.name: Duplicate Within Distance k
variations.summary: Map each value to its last-seen index and check the distance whenever the value is seen again
variations.inputShape: Array plus a distance bound k
variations.movement: Single forward pass
variations.recognizeIt: 'Two equal elements within k positions of each other' is the direct signal.
variations.keyInsight: Always overwriting with the most recent index (rather than the first) keeps the stored distance minimal and correct.

variations.name: Two Sum with Indices
variations.summary: Map each value to its index while scanning, so a found complement resolves directly into an index pair
variations.movement: Single forward pass
variations.recognizeIt: Same signal as complement-sum lookup, but the answer required is index positions, not just existence.

variations.name: Running Count by First Occurrence
variations.summary: Track how many times each value has already been seen so far, so equal earlier elements can be counted as pairs on the fly
variations.movement: Single forward pass
variations.recognizeIt: 'Count pairs where nums[i] == nums[j] and i < j' signals accumulating a running count per value rather than mapping to a single index.
variations.keyInsight: Adding the current running count before incrementing it is what turns a single pass into a correct pair count (each new match pairs with every prior occurrence).

patterns.name: Group By Computed Key
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: Uses a hash map whose values are lists (buckets), where each element is placed into a bucket based on a computed key — a normalized signature, a remainder, or a structural pattern. Elements that produce the same key end up grouped together in one linear pass, instead of comparing every pair against every other pair.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Grouping anagrams or strings with the same character makeup
Bucketing elements by a shared property like remainder or parity
Counting subarrays whose sums share a remainder
Matching strings that follow the same structural pattern
Building an index from a category or signature to its members

steps: Initialize an empty hash map to store key -> list of grouped items
Iterate through the input elements
Compute a normalized signature key for the current element (e.g. sorted characters, remainder, structural pattern)
If the key isn't in the map yet, create a new empty list for it
Append the current element to the list at that key
Once all elements are processed, return the map's grouped values as the answer

variations.name: Group Anagrams (Sorted Signature)
variations.summary: Sort each string's characters to produce a shared key, then bucket strings under that key
variations.movement: Single pass; each element hashed by its sorted form
variations.timeComplexity: O(n * k log k) where k is average string length
variations.recognizeIt: 'Group anagrams together' is the direct signal — same letters, any order, same bucket.
variations.keyInsight: Sorting a string's characters gives a canonical form: two strings are anagrams if and only if their sorted forms are identical.

variations.name: Group by Remainder (Prefix Sum Mod k)
variations.summary: Bucket running prefix-sum remainders by (sum % k) to count subarrays whose sum is divisible by k
variations.inputShape: Array of integers plus a divisor k
variations.movement: Single pass accumulating a prefix sum
variations.recognizeIt: 'Subarray sum divisible by k' or 'pairs summing to a multiple of k' signal bucketing by remainder rather than by raw value.
variations.keyInsight: Two prefix sums with the same remainder mod k mean the subarray between them sums to a multiple of k, so counting remainder collisions directly counts valid subarrays.

variations.name: Group/Match by Structural Pattern Signature
variations.summary: Normalize each string into a signature representing which letters map to which positions, then use it as a hash key to find structural matches
variations.inputShape: Array of strings plus a target pattern
variations.movement: Single pass; each string hashed by its normalized structure
variations.recognizeIt: 'Words that follow the same pattern' (e.g. abb ~ mee) signals normalizing by first-occurrence order rather than by literal characters.
variations.keyInsight: Mapping each character to the index of its first appearance produces a signature that's invariant to which specific letters are used, only their repetition structure.

patterns.name: DFS (Depth-First Search)
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: Depth-first search explores a tree by going as deep as possible down one branch before backtracking to try others. Starting at the root, it fully recurses into one child's subtree before moving to the next. It can be implemented with the natural function call stack (recursive) or an explicit stack (iterative). Preorder, inorder, and postorder are all DFS — the only difference is when, relative to the two recursive calls, a node's value gets processed.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Visiting or printing every node in a specific order
Serializing and deserializing a tree
Evaluating expression trees
Cloning or copying a tree structure
Computing a value at a node that depends on its full subtree
Producing a sorted sequence from a BST
Enumerating root-to-leaf paths

steps: Define a recursive function that accepts the current node
Base case: if the node is None, return immediately
(Preorder hook) optionally process the node's value here, before recursing
Recurse into the left child
(Inorder hook) optionally process the node's value here, between the two recursive calls
Recurse into the right child
(Postorder hook) optionally process the node's value here, after both recursive calls return
stepByStepAlgorithm.code:
def dfs(node, result):
    if node is None:
        return
    val, left, right = node
    # Preorder hook would go here
    dfs(left, result)
    # Inorder hook would go here
    dfs(right, result)
    # Postorder hook would go here
    return result

variations.name: Preorder Traversal (Root → Left → Right)
variations.summary: Process the node before recursing into either child
variations.movement: Root first, then left subtree, then right subtree
variations.spaceComplexity: O(h) recursion stack, h = tree height
variations.recognizeIt: You need a parent's value available before its children are handled — copying/serializing a tree top-down, or building a prefix-style output. 'Visit node, then children' is preorder.
variations.code:
def preorder_traversal(root):
    result = []
    def dfs(node):
        if node is None:
            return
        val, left, right = node
        result.append(val)
        dfs(left)
        dfs(right)
    dfs(root)
    return result

variations.name: Inorder Traversal (Left → Root → Right)
variations.summary: Process the node between its two subtrees
variations.movement: Left subtree, then root, then right subtree
variations.recognizeIt: On a BST this always yields ascending sorted order — recognize it whenever a problem wants nodes in sorted order, or the k-th smallest element.
variations.code:
def inorder_traversal(root):
    result = []
    def dfs(node):
        if node is None:
            return
        val, left, right = node
        dfs(left)
        result.append(val)
        dfs(right)
    dfs(root)
    return result
variations.keyInsight: For a BST specifically, inorder traversal always visits nodes in ascending order — that's the whole reason it's the standard tool for BST validation and k-th-smallest problems.

variations.name: Postorder Traversal (Left → Right → Root)
variations.summary: Process the node only after both children are fully done
variations.movement: Left subtree, then right subtree, then root
variations.recognizeIt: Needed whenever a node's answer depends on results already computed from both children — deleting a tree bottom-up, evaluating expression trees, or any bottom-up DP over a tree.
variations.code:
def postorder_traversal(root):
    result = []
    def dfs(node):
        if node is None:
            return
        val, left, right = node
        dfs(left)
        dfs(right)
        result.append(val)
    dfs(root)
    return result
variations.keyInsight: Postorder is the natural order for any 'compute child results first' recursion — height, diameter, and subtree-sum patterns are all postorder underneath.

variations.name: Iterative DFS with an Explicit Stack
variations.summary: Simulate the recursion manually with your own stack to avoid call-stack depth limits
variations.movement: Same root/left/right ordering as recursive DFS, driven by a while loop popping from a stack
variations.spaceComplexity: O(h), O(n) worst case for a skewed tree
variations.recognizeIt: Recognize when a problem forbids recursion, or the tree can be deep enough to blow the call stack (e.g. a skewed tree with 10^5 nodes).
variations.keyInsight: Pushing the right child before the left child reproduces preorder order when popping, because the stack is LIFO.

patterns.name: BFS (Breadth-First Search) / Level Order Traversal
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: Breadth-first search explores a tree level by level, visiting every node at depth d before any node at depth d+1. It's implemented with a queue: push the root, then repeatedly pop a node, process it, and push its children — which naturally processes nodes in level order. Because a whole level finishes before the next starts, BFS is the standard tool for anything measured 'level by level', or for the shortest path from the root in an unweighted tree.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Level-order traversal / printing a tree row by row
Finding the minimum depth or shortest root-to-leaf path
Zigzag / spiral level traversal
Getting the leftmost or rightmost visible node at each level
Connecting nodes at the same level (next pointers)
Level-by-level aggregation (sums, max, averages per level)

steps: Create a queue and push the root node onto it (if root is not null)
Loop while the queue is not empty
Record the current queue length as the number of nodes in this level
Pop that many nodes from the front of the queue, processing each
For each popped node, push its non-null children onto the queue
After the inner loop, one full level has been processed
Continue until the queue is empty

variations.name: Standard Level Order
variations.summary: Group node values level by level, left to right
variations.movement: Queue-driven, one full level at a time, top to bottom
variations.spaceComplexity: O(w), w = max width of the tree
variations.recognizeIt: The problem literally asks for 'level order' output, or wants per-level aggregates like sums, max, or averages.

variations.name: Zigzag (Spiral) Level Order
variations.summary: Alternate direction each level: left-to-right, then right-to-left
variations.movement: Same queue mechanics as level order, reversing alternate levels' output
variations.recognizeIt: The problem explicitly says 'zigzag' or 'spiral' order between consecutive levels.
variations.keyInsight: No special traversal is needed — run standard BFS and just reverse every other level's collected list before appending it.

variations.name: Right (or Left) Side View
variations.summary: Keep only the last (or first) node encountered at each level
variations.movement: Queue-driven level order, but only the boundary node per level is kept
variations.recognizeIt: The problem asks what nodes you'd see standing on the right/left side of the tree — that's exactly the last/first element of each BFS level.
variations.keyInsight: An equivalent recursive version does preorder with (right, left) order and records only the first node seen at each depth.

variations.name: Minimum Depth (Shortest Root-to-Leaf Path)
variations.summary: BFS stops as soon as the first leaf is found, since it explores level by level
variations.movement: Queue-driven level order, early-exits on first leaf
variations.recognizeIt: The problem wants the shortest path or minimum depth to any leaf — BFS often terminates early, unlike DFS which must explore every path to be sure.
variations.keyInsight: Unlike max depth (which needs to explore everything via DFS), min depth benefits from BFS: the first leaf hit in level order is guaranteed to be at the minimum depth.

patterns.name: Top Down
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: In a top-down recursive pattern, information flows from parent to child as extra function arguments — a running sum, a valid range, a max-so-far value, or the current depth. Each child combines that inherited state with its own value to decide what to do, or what to pass further down. The base case (usually a null node or a leaf) is often where the final answer for that path gets recorded. It's the mirror image of bottom-up: state flows down, not up.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Root-to-leaf path problems (sums, longest path, path existence)
Validating constraints that depend on ancestors, like BST validity
Counting nodes at a given depth or with a given ancestor property
Tracking the max/min value seen so far along a path
Propagating a running total down to leaves

steps: Define a recursive helper taking the current node plus state accumulated from the parent
Base case: if the node is null, return or finalize using the accumulated state
Combine the current node's value with the incoming state to produce updated state
Recurse into the left child, passing the updated state
Recurse into the right child, passing the updated state
If the node is a leaf, this is often where the answer for this path gets recorded

variations.name: Root-to-Leaf Path Sum
variations.summary: Carry a running sum down and check/record it once a leaf is reached
variations.inputShape: Binary tree with numeric node values
variations.movement: Top-down; state = accumulated sum so far
variations.recognizeIt: The problem mentions summing values along a root-to-leaf path, or checking whether such a sum equals a target.

variations.name: Validate BST via Min/Max Range
variations.summary: Pass down the valid (low, high) range each node's value must fall in
variations.inputShape: Binary tree to be checked for the BST property
variations.movement: Top-down; state = the (low, high) bound inherited from ancestors
variations.recognizeIt: The problem asks whether a tree is a valid BST — the trap is that comparing only to the immediate parent isn't enough; you need the range imposed by ALL ancestors.
variations.keyInsight: A node isn't bounded just by its direct parent — it's bounded by the tightest range imposed by all ancestors combined, which is exactly what gets threaded top-down.

variations.name: Max/Min Ancestor Value Along a Path
variations.summary: Carry the best (max or min) value seen so far down each path
variations.movement: Top-down; state = max (or min) value seen among ancestors
variations.recognizeIt: The problem compares a node to its ancestors, e.g. 'largest difference between a node and one of its ancestors'.

variations.name: Does a Root-to-Leaf Path With Target Sum Exist
variations.summary: Boolean version of path sum — short-circuit as soon as one matching path is found
variations.inputShape: Binary tree with numeric node values
variations.movement: Top-down; state = remaining target after subtracting ancestors' values
variations.timeComplexity: O(n) worst case, often less due to short-circuiting
variations.recognizeIt: The problem only asks for existence (yes/no), not enumeration of every path — so you can return as soon as one match is found instead of collecting all sums.
variations.keyInsight: Subtracting the node's value from the remaining target as you descend means a leaf only needs to check remaining == 0, instead of tracking and comparing the full running sum.

patterns.name: Bottom Up
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: In bottom-up (postorder) recursion, each node first calls itself on its children, waits for their results, and only then computes and returns its own answer using those results. The parent never hands information down; instead information flows upward from leaves to root. This is the natural approach when a node's answer literally requires knowing something about its subtrees first, such as their height, sum, or validity. The recursive call for a node completes only after both child calls have completed, so leaves are effectively resolved before their ancestors.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Computing a node's answer from its children's already-solved answers (height, sum, count)
Checking a global structural property that requires comparing left and right subtree results (balanced, symmetric)
Combining two independent subtree results into one value at each node
Propagating an 'this subtree is invalid' signal upward without extra passes
Building or pruning a tree based on information only known after visiting children
Any problem where the answer for a subtree can't be known until both children are fully solved

steps: Define a recursive helper that takes a node and returns whatever the subtree needs to report upward
Base case: if node is null, return the identity value for this problem (0, true, empty, etc.)
Recursively call the helper on the left child and store its result
Recursively call the helper on the right child and store its result
Combine the left result, right result, and the current node's own value into this node's result
Optionally update a global/outer variable here if the problem needs a running best answer across all nodes
Return the combined result so the parent call can use it

variations.name: Aggregate Return (count / sum)
variations.summary: Each call returns a single number built from its children's numbers plus its own node
variations.movement: Leaves upward to root, one combined number per call
variations.recognizeIt: Question asks for a single count/sum/total across the whole tree (nodes, leaves, path sums) — the answer is just 'add up something from every node,' and each subtree's mini-total is exactly what its parent needs.

variations.name: Multi-Value Tuple Return (balanced + height)
variations.summary: Each call returns a tuple/struct bundling more than one fact about the subtree
variations.movement: Leaves upward, packing (height, isBalanced) or similar pairs
variations.recognizeIt: The check needs two related facts about a subtree at once (its height AND whether it's balanced) — computing them in two separate passes would multiply costs, so bundle both into one return value per call.
variations.keyInsight: Returning a tuple avoids recomputing height in a separate pass for every node, dropping the naive O(n^2) balanced-check down to O(n).

variations.name: Global Accumulator + Local Return (count univalue subtrees)
variations.summary: Each call returns a value the parent needs, while separately updating an outer counter/best-so-far as a side effect
variations.movement: Leaves upward; a nonlocal/class variable accumulates across all calls
variations.recognizeIt: Problem wants a count/best value across the WHOLE tree, but that count isn't naturally the thing a parent needs back from a child — the child needs to return something else (e.g. 'is this subtree univalue') while quietly bumping a separate running total.
variations.keyInsight: The returned value (is-univalue) and the tracked answer (how many univalue subtrees exist) are different quantities — don't force one function's return type to carry both jobs.

variations.name: Prune / Modify Bottom-Up (delete leaves with target value)
variations.summary: Each call rebuilds/prunes its own subtree using the already-pruned children before deciding its own fate
variations.inputShape: Any binary tree, mutated or rebuilt in place
variations.movement: Leaves upward; children are fixed before parent decides whether to remove itself
variations.recognizeIt: Task says 'remove/prune nodes matching a condition that depends on children's state' — a node can only know if it should be removed after its children have already been cleaned up.

patterns.name: Maximum Path Sum
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: This pattern finds the path through a binary tree with the largest sum, where a path is any sequence of nodes connected by edges and doesn't have to pass through the root or end at a leaf. At every node, you compute the best single-branch contribution that node can offer upward (its own value plus the better of its two children, clipped at zero), while separately checking whether routing through both children at once beats the best answer found so far.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Best path sum between any two nodes in a tree
Longest/heaviest downward or through-node path
Diameter-style problems that combine two subtree extremes at a node
Counting paths whose sum matches a target
Any 'best path, can start/end anywhere' variant on trees or tree-shaped graphs

steps: Keep a global/nonlocal variable best_sum initialized to negative infinity
Define a recursive helper max_gain(node) returning the best sum a single downward path starting at node can contribute
Base case: a null node contributes 0
Recursively get left_gain = max_gain(node.left), clipped to 0 if negative (a path is never forced to include a bad branch)
Recursively get right_gain = max_gain(node.right), clipped to 0 the same way
Update best_sum = max(best_sum, node.val + left_gain + right_gain), which considers the path THROUGH this node using both children
Return node.val + max(left_gain, right_gain), the best a caller one level up can extend, using only ONE branch
After recursion finishes, best_sum holds the answer
stepByStepAlgorithm.code:
def max_path_sum(root):
    best_sum = float("-inf")

    def max_gain(node):
        nonlocal best_sum
        if node is None:
            return 0

        left_gain = max(max_gain(node.left), 0)
        right_gain = max(max_gain(node.right), 0)

        # best path if it passes THROUGH this node using both children
        best_sum = max(best_sum, node.val + left_gain + right_gain)

        # best path a parent can extend, using only one branch
        return node.val + max(left_gain, right_gain)

    max_gain(root)
    return best_sum

variations.name: Root-to-Leaf Max Path Sum
variations.summary: Restricts the path to start at the root and end at any leaf
variations.inputShape: Binary tree, possibly with negative values
variations.movement: Top-down accumulation, compared only at leaves
variations.recognizeIt: The phrase 'root to leaf' pins both endpoints, so this is solvable top-down (carry a running sum) or bottom-up (return the best single-branch sum) — no through-node combination step is needed.
variations.keyInsight: Unlike the general pattern, there is no 'through-node using both branches' case here — a root-to-leaf path can only ever go down one side at each step.

variations.name: Any Node to Any Node (classic Max Path Sum)
variations.summary: The general case: the path can start and end anywhere, and may or may not pass through the root
variations.inputShape: Binary tree, values can be negative
variations.movement: Bottom-up gain calculation with a global best updated through-node
variations.recognizeIt: No constraint on where the path starts or ends — 'maximum path sum' with no other qualifiers is the signal for the full through-node combination pattern.
variations.keyInsight: Clipping negative branch gains to 0 is what lets a node 'opt out' of a bad subtree instead of being forced to include it.

variations.name: Count Paths Summing to Target (downward, not necessarily through root)
variations.summary: Instead of one max value, count how many downward paths sum exactly to a target, typically combined with a prefix-sum hashmap
variations.inputShape: Binary tree with a target integer
variations.movement: Top-down with a running prefix sum and a hashmap
variations.timeComplexity: O(n) with the prefix-sum hashmap, O(n^2) worst case with a brute-force downward scan
variations.recognizeIt: The problem asks 'how many paths sum to target,' not 'what's the maximum' — this counting variant usually pairs tree recursion with the prefix-sum trick borrowed from array problems.
variations.code:
def path_sum_count(root, target_sum):
    prefix_counts = {0: 1}
    count = 0

    def helper(node, current_sum):
        nonlocal count
        if node is None:
            return
        current_sum += node.val
        count += prefix_counts.get(current_sum - target_sum, 0)
        prefix_counts[current_sum] = prefix_counts.get(current_sum, 0) + 1

        helper(node.left, current_sum)
        helper(node.right, current_sum)

        prefix_counts[current_sum] -= 1  # backtrack

    helper(root, 0)
    return count
variations.keyInsight: The prefix-sum hashmap must be backtracked (decremented) when leaving a node, or sibling subtrees will incorrectly see each other's sums.

patterns.name: Diameter / Height / Depth
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: This pattern computes a single-pass, bottom-up height function that returns each subtree's height, while smuggling a diameter (or similar cross-node) computation on the side. The key idea is that a tree's diameter — the longest path between any two nodes — is, at some node, exactly the sum of its left and right subtree heights; you never need a second traversal to find it if you fold the comparison into the same recursive call that already computes height.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Diameter of a binary tree (longest path between any two nodes)
Height / maximum depth of a tree
Minimum depth to the nearest leaf
Checking whether a tree is height-balanced using the same single pass
Any 'longest/shortest path measured in edges or nodes' question on a tree

steps: Keep a global/nonlocal variable (e.g. diameter) initialized to 0
Define a recursive helper height(node) that returns the height of the subtree rooted at node
Base case: a null node has height 0
Recursively compute left_height = height(node.left) and right_height = height(node.right)
Update diameter = max(diameter, left_height + right_height), since the longest path THROUGH this node is the sum of its two subtree heights
Return 1 + max(left_height, right_height) as this node's height to the caller
After the recursion completes, diameter holds the final answer

variations.name: Maximum Depth / Height
variations.summary: Plain bottom-up height computation with no side accumulator needed
variations.movement: Leaves upward, one integer per call
variations.recognizeIt: The question asks only for 'depth' or 'height' of the whole tree with nothing about paths between two arbitrary nodes — the simplest member of this family, requiring no global variable.

variations.name: Minimum Depth to Nearest Leaf
variations.summary: Same shape as max depth, but must special-case single-child nodes so they aren't mistaken for leaves
variations.movement: Leaves upward, but a one-sided subtree doesn't count as a valid stopping point
variations.recognizeIt: Problem says 'minimum depth' or 'nearest leaf' — the trap is that a node with only one child isn't a leaf, so naively taking min(left,right)+1 undercounts through empty branches.
variations.keyInsight: Without the one-sided-child guards, a node with a single child would incorrectly report depth 1 through its missing side, even though that side isn't a real leaf.
