variations.name: Diameter (longest path through any node)
variations.summary: The main pattern: height computation with a diameter side-accumulator
variations.movement: Leaves upward; diameter tracked as sum of left/right heights at each node
variations.recognizeIt: The question says 'diameter' or 'longest path between any two nodes' — always solvable in the same single pass that computes height, by comparing left+right heights at every node.
variations.keyInsight: The diameter doesn't have to pass through the root — that's exactly why it's tracked as a running max across every node, not just computed once at the top.

variations.name: Balanced Check via Height (single pass, early-exit sentinel)
variations.summary: Reuses the height recursion but returns a sentinel (-1) the moment imbalance is detected, avoiding a full second pass
variations.movement: Leaves upward; a -1 sentinel short-circuits further work
variations.recognizeIt: This is the same 'balanced binary tree' question as the multi-value-tuple-return variation under Bottom Up, but reframed to smuggle the boolean through a single integer height using an impossible sentinel value instead of a tuple — a useful alternative to bundling worth recognizing on its own.
variations.keyInsight: Using -1 as an 'impossible height' sentinel lets one integer carry both the real height and a poisoned/imbalanced signal, avoiding a tuple return entirely.

patterns.name: BST Property-Based Operations
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: A Binary Search Tree keeps every left subtree strictly less than its node and every right subtree strictly greater, which turns tree operations into a series of one-way decisions instead of a full traversal. At each node you compare the target to the node's value and immediately know which single subtree could possibly contain the answer, discarding the other half of the tree. This is what lets search, insert, and delete run in O(height) time rather than O(n). Many BST-specific problems are really this same left/right comparison trick reused for a different goal: finding a value, ranking values via inorder order, or finding where two paths diverge.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Searching for a value in O(height) instead of O(n)
Inserting or deleting a node while preserving BST ordering
Finding the Kth smallest or largest value without full sorting
Finding the lowest common ancestor of two nodes efficiently
Validating whether a tree actually satisfies BST ordering everywhere
Range-based queries (counting/summing values between two bounds)

steps: Start at the root node
If the current node is null, you've hit a dead end — handle the base case (not found, insertion point, etc.)
Compare the target value with the current node's value
If they're equal, this is the node you're looking for — process/return it
If the target is smaller, the answer (if it exists) must be in the left subtree — move left and discard the right subtree entirely
If the target is larger, move right and discard the left subtree entirely
Repeat until the target is found or you fall off the tree (null)
stepByStepAlgorithm.code:
def bst_operation(root, target):
    """
    Generic template for BST property-based descent.
    Demonstrates the core left/right pruning decision shared by
    search, insert-position-finding, and floor/ceiling style problems.
    """
    current = root
    while current is not None:
        if target == current.val:
            # Found it - process and return
            return current
        elif target < current.val:
            # Answer can only be in the left subtree - right side pruned
            current = current.left
        else:
            # Answer can only be in the right subtree - left side pruned
            current = current.right
    # Fell off the tree without finding target
    return None

variations.name: Validate BST
variations.summary: Check that every node satisfies BST ordering against ancestor bounds, not just its immediate children
variations.inputShape: Binary tree, checking global BST property
variations.movement: Top-down, carrying (low, high) bounds down each recursive call
variations.recognizeIt: Problem says 'validate' or 'is this a valid BST' — the trap is comparing a node only to its direct children, which misses violations from a grandparent further up. Recognize you need a running (low, high) range passed down, not a local comparison.
variations.keyInsight: A node isn't valid just because it's greater than its left child and less than its right child — it must also respect every bound inherited from ancestors above it. Passing (low, high) down as you recurse encodes that inherited constraint.

variations.name: Search / Insert / Delete in BST
variations.summary: Use the BST property to locate a value's position, then insert or remove while keeping ordering intact
variations.inputShape: BST root + a value to search, insert, or delete
variations.movement: Descend left/right based on comparison; delete requires an extra step to find a replacement node
variations.timeComplexity: O(h), where h is tree height
variations.recognizeIt: Problem explicitly says 'insert into a BST', 'delete a node', or 'search in a BST' — the giveaway is that ordering must be preserved after the mutation, not just after the read.
variations.keyInsight: Deleting a two-child node is the hard case: you can't just remove it, so you replace its value with its inorder successor (smallest value in the right subtree) and then delete that successor instead, which is guaranteed to have at most one child.

variations.name: Kth Smallest / Largest Element
variations.summary: Use inorder traversal order to find rank-based values without sorting
variations.movement: Inorder traversal (left, node, right) counting visited nodes
variations.recognizeIt: Problem asks for the 'Kth smallest' or 'Kth largest' value — the signal is that inorder traversal of a BST visits nodes in sorted order for free, so you just need to count to k instead of sorting everything.
variations.code:
def kth_smallest(root, k):
    stack = []
    current = root
    count = 0
    while stack or current:
        while current:
            stack.append(current)
            current = current.left
        current = stack.pop()
        count += 1
        if count == k:
            return current.val
        current = current.right
    return -1
variations.keyInsight: Inorder traversal of a BST always yields values in ascending order, so the Kth smallest is simply the Kth node visited — no sorting or extra data structure needed. For Kth largest, do the mirrored right-node-left traversal instead.

variations.name: Lowest Common Ancestor in BST
variations.summary: Use BST ordering to find where two target values' paths diverge, without searching both full paths
variations.inputShape: BST root + two node values (p, q)
variations.movement: Single descent from root, turning left or right based on where p and q sit relative to the current node
variations.recognizeIt: Problem says 'lowest common ancestor' and the tree is specifically a BST (not a generic binary tree) — the signal is that you don't need to store parent paths at all, since the split point is found purely by comparing values.
variations.keyInsight: The LCA is the first node where p and q's values fall on opposite sides (or one matches exactly) — before that point both targets are on the same side, so you can safely keep descending in just one direction without checking the other subtree at all.

patterns.name: BFS (Breadth-First Search)
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: Breadth-First Search explores a graph level by level, visiting all neighbors of a node before moving on to their neighbors' neighbors. It uses a queue to process nodes in first-in-first-out order, which guarantees that in an unweighted graph, the first time a node is reached is via the shortest possible path from the source. A visited set stops it from revisiting nodes and looping forever on cyclic graphs.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Finding the shortest path in an unweighted graph
Level-order traversal of a graph or tree
Finding the minimum number of moves/steps between two states
Detecting connected components
Finding all nodes reachable within k steps
Solving shortest-path puzzles on grids (mazes)

steps: Create a queue and add the starting node; mark it visited.
While the queue is not empty, dequeue the node at the front.
Process the dequeued node (record it, check if it's the target, etc.).
For each unvisited neighbor of the dequeued node, mark it visited and enqueue it.
Repeat until the queue is empty — every reachable node has now been visited.

variations.name: Shortest path in an unweighted graph
variations.summary: BFS with a distance array to compute shortest distance from a source to every node
variations.movement: Layer by layer, outward from the source
variations.recognizeIt: The problem asks for the minimum number of edges/steps between two nodes in an unweighted graph, or the minimum number of moves between states.
variations.keyInsight: Because BFS visits nodes in strictly increasing order of distance from the source, the first time a node is dequeued its distance is already final — no relaxation step is needed like in Dijkstra.

variations.name: BFS on a 2D grid
variations.summary: Treat grid cells as graph nodes and 4- (or 8-) directional neighbors as edges
variations.movement: Expands outward to adjacent up/down/left/right (or diagonal) cells
variations.recognizeIt: The problem gives a grid/matrix and asks for minimum steps, shortest path, or a flood-fill-style spread (e.g. rot, distance to nearest zero).
variations.keyInsight: Encode each cell as (row, col) and reuse the exact same visited/queue logic as graph BFS — the grid IS the adjacency list, just computed on the fly instead of stored.

variations.name: Level-by-level BFS (track depth)
variations.summary: Process the queue in whole levels, using a snapshot of the queue size to know how many nodes belong to the current level
variations.movement: One full level is dequeued before the next level begins
variations.recognizeIt: The problem needs a per-level result (level-order traversal, minimum depth, zigzag order) rather than just the overall visiting order.
variations.keyInsight: Snapshot len(queue) at the start of each while-loop iteration, before enqueuing any children — that count is exactly how many nodes belong to the current level.

patterns.name: DFS (Depth-First Search)
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: Depth-First Search explores a graph by going as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking, using recursion (or an explicit stack) to remember where to return. It's the natural fit for exploring all paths, uncovering structure like cycles or connected components, or when a branch needs to fully unwind before the next one is tried. A visited set stops it from looping forever on cyclic graphs.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Finding connected components
Detecting cycles
Topological sorting
Path existence / enumerating all paths between two nodes
Counting islands / connected regions in a grid
Backtracking-style exploration of graph structures

steps: Mark the starting node as visited.
Process the current node (record it, check a condition, etc.).
For each unvisited neighbor, recursively call DFS on it (or push it onto an explicit stack).
When a node has no more unvisited neighbors, backtrack to its caller.
Repeat until every reachable node has been visited.

variations.name: Recursive DFS traversal
variations.summary: Classic recursive DFS using the call stack
variations.movement: Deepest unvisited branch first, then backtrack
variations.recognizeIt: The problem just needs to visit/explore all nodes, check connectivity, or enumerate paths — order along a branch matters more than shortest distance.
variations.keyInsight: The call stack implicitly stores the path back to the root — no explicit stack is needed unless recursion depth risks a stack overflow on very large graphs.

variations.name: Iterative DFS with an explicit stack
variations.summary: Simulate recursion using your own stack to avoid recursion-depth limits
variations.movement: LIFO stack pops the most recently pushed node
variations.recognizeIt: The graph could be very large or deep, and a recursive solution risks hitting the language's recursion-depth limit.
variations.keyInsight: Push neighbors in reverse order if you need the exact same visiting order as the recursive version — a plain stack pop reverses the natural iteration order.

variations.name: Counting / labeling connected components
variations.summary: Run DFS from every unvisited node, incrementing a counter each time a fresh DFS starts
variations.movement: One full DFS per unvisited seed node
variations.recognizeIt: The problem asks 'how many separate groups / islands / provinces' exist in the structure.
variations.keyInsight: Each call to DFS from an unvisited node discovers exactly one full component — the number of times you start a fresh DFS is the answer, no separate counting logic needed.

patterns.name: Cycle Detection (Directed Graph)
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: Detecting a cycle in a directed graph means checking whether following edges can ever lead back to a node that's currently being explored. The standard approach tracks not just which nodes have ever been visited, but which nodes are on the current DFS recursion path — hitting a node already on that path means you've found a back edge, and therefore a cycle. Kahn's algorithm offers a non-recursive alternative: if you can't produce a topological order covering every node, a cycle must exist.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Detecting deadlocks in resource-allocation graphs
Validating that a set of prerequisites/tasks has no circular dependency
Checking whether a directed graph is a DAG before running topological sort
Detecting infinite loops in state-transition graphs
Build-system / package-manager dependency-cycle checks

steps: Maintain two arrays: visited (ever explored) and on_stack (currently on the recursion path).
For each unvisited node, start a DFS.
Mark the node visited and add it to on_stack.
For each neighbor: if it's on on_stack, a cycle is found; if it's unvisited, recurse into it.
Before returning from the DFS call, remove the node from on_stack (backtrack).
If no back edge is ever found across all starting nodes, the graph is acyclic.

variations.name: DFS with recursion-stack tracking
variations.summary: Track nodes on the current DFS path; a back edge to one of them means a cycle
variations.movement: Depth-first, tracking path membership as it goes
variations.recognizeIt: Directed graph, and you need a yes/no cycle answer (or the actual cycle) rather than a full ordering.
variations.keyInsight: A cycle in a directed graph only exists if you hit a node currently on your OWN recursion path — hitting an already-visited-but-off-stack node is fine, it's just two paths converging, not a cycle.

variations.name: Kahn's algorithm cycle check
variations.summary: Run Kahn's topological sort; if fewer than V nodes get processed, a cycle exists
variations.inputShape: Directed graph (adjacency list) with in-degree counts
variations.movement: BFS consuming zero-in-degree nodes
variations.recognizeIt: You need cycle detection without recursion, or you're already computing a topological order and want the cycle check for free.
variations.code:
from collections import deque, defaultdict

def find_order(num_courses, prerequisites):
    graph = defaultdict(list)
    in_degree = [0] * num_courses

    for a, b in prerequisites:
        graph[b].append(a)
        in_degree[a] += 1

    queue = deque([c for c in range(num_courses) if in_degree[c] == 0])
    order = []

    while queue:
        node = queue.popleft()
        order.append(node)
        for neighbor in graph[node]:
            in_degree[neighbor] -= 1
            if in_degree[neighbor] == 0:
                queue.append(neighbor)

    return order if len(order) == num_courses else []
variations.keyInsight: Nodes that are part of a cycle never reach in-degree zero, so they're never enqueued — the leftover, un-enqueued count directly reveals a cycle.

patterns.name: Cycle Detection - Undirected Graph
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: To find a cycle in an undirected graph, traverse it (via DFS or BFS) while remembering, for each visited node, which neighbor you arrived from (its parent in the traversal). If you ever reach a node that is already visited and it is NOT the immediate parent you just came from, you've found a back-edge, which means a cycle exists. This parent-tracking is what distinguishes undirected cycle detection from the directed case, since in an undirected graph every edge naturally 'points back' to where you came from and must be excluded from counting as a cycle.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Detecting whether an undirected graph contains any cycle
Verifying if a graph is a valid tree (connected + acyclic)
Counting the minimum edges to remove to make a graph acyclic
Deciding if adding one more edge/connection creates a redundant cycle
Checking network topologies for closed loops

steps: Build an adjacency list from the edge list, adding each edge in both directions.
Create a visited array of size n, initialized to False.
For every unvisited node, start a DFS passing (node, parent=-1).
Inside DFS, mark the current node visited, then loop over its neighbors.
If a neighbor is unvisited, recurse into it with the current node as its parent; if that recursive call reports a cycle, propagate True upward immediately.
If a neighbor IS visited and is not equal to the current node's parent, a cycle has been found -- return True.
If the loop finishes with no cycle found, return False for this branch.
After checking all components, if no DFS call ever returned True, the graph is acyclic.

variations.name: DFS with parent tracking
variations.summary: Recursive depth-first search that carries the parent node along and flags any visited non-parent neighbor as a cycle
variations.inputShape: Undirected graph, adjacency list or edge list
variations.movement: Depth-first, one branch fully explored before backtracking
variations.recognizeIt: Undirected edges, need a single yes/no cycle answer, and the graph is small/medium enough that recursion depth is not a concern.
variations.keyInsight: Excluding only the direct parent (not all previously-visited nodes) is what correctly ignores the trivial 'bounce back on the same edge' case while still catching real cycles.

variations.name: BFS with parent tracking
variations.summary: Iterative level-order traversal using a queue of (node, parent) pairs, avoiding recursion depth limits
variations.inputShape: Undirected graph, adjacency list or edge list
variations.movement: Level by level, outward from each unvisited component's start node
variations.recognizeIt: Same detection goal as the DFS version, but preferred when the graph could be deep/large and you want to avoid recursion stack limits.

patterns.name: Topological Sort
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: Topological sort produces a linear ordering of a directed acyclic graph's (DAG) vertices such that for every edge u -> v, u comes before v in the ordering. It only makes sense on DAGs -- if a cycle exists, no valid ordering can exist, so the same process can also be used to detect cycles. There are two standard ways to build this ordering: Kahn's algorithm, which repeatedly removes vertices with no remaining incoming edges, and a DFS-based approach, which records vertices in the reverse order they finish being explored.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Ordering tasks/courses that have prerequisite dependencies
Determining a valid build/compile order for modules
Detecting whether a set of dependencies contains an impossible cycle
Scheduling jobs where some jobs must run before others
Deriving an alphabet's letter order from sorted word lists

steps: Build an adjacency list and compute the in-degree (count of incoming edges) for every vertex.
Initialize a queue with all vertices whose in-degree is 0.
While the queue is not empty, pop a vertex and append it to the result order.
For each neighbor of the popped vertex, decrement its in-degree by 1.
If a neighbor's in-degree drops to 0, push it onto the queue.
Repeat until the queue is empty.
If the result order contains all vertices, it is a valid topological order; otherwise a cycle exists and no valid order does.

variations.name: Kahn's Algorithm (BFS + In-degree)
variations.summary: Iteratively remove vertices with zero in-degree using a BFS-style queue
variations.inputShape: DAG as adjacency list + edge list
variations.movement: Layer by layer -- the zero-in-degree frontier expands outward
variations.recognizeIt: Problem mentions 'prerequisites' or 'dependencies' and wants a processing order or a cycle-existence flag; naturally iterative, easy to reason about.
variations.keyInsight: A cycle exists exactly when the final order has fewer vertices than the graph -- those leftover vertices' in-degree never reached zero because they're stuck depending on each other.

variations.name: DFS-based Topological Sort
variations.summary: Run DFS and record each vertex in post-order (after all its descendants are done), then reverse
variations.movement: Depth-first; ordering comes from finish time, not discovery time
variations.recognizeIt: A recursive/DFS-friendly formulation, or when you also want to explicitly flag back-edges as part of the same traversal (e.g. combined cycle detection + ordering).
variations.keyInsight: A vertex is only safe to place in the order once every vertex reachable from it has already been placed -- reversed post-order gives exactly that for free.

patterns.name: Dijkstra's Algorithm
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: Dijkstra's algorithm finds the shortest distance from a single source to every other vertex in a graph with non-negative edge weights. It works greedily: always expand the not-yet-finalized vertex with the smallest known tentative distance, using a min-heap to pick that vertex efficiently, and relax its outgoing edges. Because weights are non-negative, once a vertex is popped from the heap with its true shortest distance, that distance can never be improved later, so each vertex is finalized exactly once.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Finding shortest travel time/cost from one source to all nodes
Network delay / signal propagation time problems
Shortest path in weighted road networks or flight routes
Path problems where the 'cost' to minimize is a max-edge or similar non-standard combine rule
Maximizing product-based path metrics like probability of success

steps: Initialize a distance array with infinity for every vertex except the source, which is 0.
Push (0, source) onto a min-heap priority queue.
While the heap is not empty, pop the entry with the smallest tentative distance.
If this popped distance is greater than the vertex's currently recorded distance, it's stale -- skip it.
Otherwise, for each neighbor, compute the distance through the current vertex.
If that new distance is smaller than the neighbor's recorded distance, update it and push the neighbor onto the heap.
Repeat until the heap is empty; the distance array now holds the shortest distance from the source to every vertex.

variations.name: Basic Dijkstra with min-heap
variations.summary: Classic single-source shortest distance using a priority queue
variations.inputShape: Weighted graph, all edge weights non-negative, adjacency list
variations.movement: Greedy expansion in order of smallest known distance
variations.recognizeIt: Weighted graph with only non-negative weights, and you need shortest distance from one source to all (or one) other node.
variations.keyInsight: Once a vertex is popped with its true shortest distance it never needs revisiting -- non-negative weights guarantee no cheaper path can appear later.

variations.name: Dijkstra with path reconstruction
variations.summary: Same relaxation, but also records each vertex's predecessor to rebuild the actual shortest path
variations.inputShape: Weighted graph plus an explicit source and target
variations.movement: Same greedy expansion, then backtrack once the target is settled
variations.recognizeIt: The problem asks for the actual sequence of nodes/edges in the shortest path, not just its total length.
variations.code:
import heapq

def dijkstra_with_path(num_vertices, edges, source, target):
    graph = [[] for _ in range(num_vertices)]
    for u, v, w in edges:
        graph[u].append((v, w))
        graph[v].append((u, w))

    dist = [float('inf')] * num_vertices
    dist[source] = 0
    prev = [-1] * num_vertices
    heap = [(0, source)]

    while heap:
        d, node = heapq.heappop(heap)
        if d > dist[node]:
            continue
        for neighbor, weight in graph[node]:
            new_dist = d + weight
            if new_dist < dist[neighbor]:
                dist[neighbor] = new_dist
                prev[neighbor] = node
                heapq.heappush(heap, (new_dist, neighbor))

    if dist[target] == float('inf'):
        return []

    path = []
    node = target
    while node != -1:
        path.append(node)
        node = prev[node]
    return path[::-1]
variations.keyInsight: Storing exactly one predecessor per vertex, updated only when its distance improves, is enough to reconstruct the full path by walking backward from the target.

variations.name: Dijkstra on an implicit grid graph
variations.summary: Applies the same relaxation logic to a grid, where edges to the 4 neighbors are generated on the fly instead of stored explicitly
variations.inputShape: 2D grid/matrix where each cell is a node connected to its 4 (or 8) neighbors
variations.movement: Same greedy priority-queue expansion, but neighbors come from row/col offsets
variations.recognizeIt: The graph is a grid/matrix, and building an explicit adjacency list would be wasteful -- neighbors are computed from coordinates instead.
variations.keyInsight: Dijkstra's greedy correctness still holds if you swap the relaxation rule (sum, max-of-edges, product) as long as the combining operator is monotonic non-decreasing.

patterns.name: Bellman-Ford Algorithm
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: Bellman-Ford computes shortest distances from a single source even when the graph has negative edge weights, which Dijkstra cannot safely handle. It works by relaxing every edge in the graph, repeated exactly V-1 times, since any shortest path can use at most V-1 edges. Running one additional relaxation pass afterward can detect whether a negative-weight cycle exists, since a true shortest-path graph should never have any edge left that can still be relaxed.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Shortest path in graphs that contain negative edge weights
Detecting negative-weight cycles reachable from a source (or anywhere in the graph)
Cheapest path problems with a limited number of allowed edges/stops
Currency arbitrage detection (negative cycle in log-weighted exchange graph)
Routing problems where some edges represent costs/gains that can be negative

steps: Initialize a distance array with infinity for every vertex except the source, which is 0.
Repeat the relaxation process exactly (V - 1) times, where V is the number of vertices.
In each pass, for every edge (u, v, weight), if dist[u] + weight < dist[v], update dist[v].
After (V - 1) passes, all shortest distances are correct, provided no negative cycle is reachable from the source.
Run one additional pass over every edge.
If any edge can still be relaxed in that extra pass, a negative-weight cycle exists and distances are unreliable.
Otherwise, return the finalized distance array.

variations.name: Standard Bellman-Ford (Edge Relaxation)
variations.summary: Relax every edge V-1 times in a simple loop -- no priority queue needed
variations.inputShape: Weighted graph, possibly with negative edges, given as an edge list
variations.movement: Global sweeps over every edge, repeated V-1 times
variations.recognizeIt: The graph may contain negative edge weights (ruling out Dijkstra), and the input is naturally an edge list rather than needing a heap.
variations.keyInsight: After k passes, every shortest path using at most k edges is already correct -- so V-1 passes are always enough, since a simple shortest path visits at most V-1 edges.

variations.name: Bellman-Ford with Negative Cycle Detection
variations.summary: Seeds every vertex at distance 0 (not just the source) so any negative cycle anywhere in the graph is caught, not only ones reachable from a single start
variations.movement: Same V-1 relaxation sweeps, plus a final detection pass
variations.recognizeIt: The question only asks whether a negative cycle exists anywhere in the graph, not for actual shortest distances from a specific source.
variations.keyInsight: Initializing every vertex to distance 0 is equivalent to adding a virtual source with 0-weight edges to everything -- it lets one Bellman-Ford run check the whole graph instead of just nodes reachable from a single source.

variations.name: SPFA optimization (Queue-based)
variations.summary: Only re-processes vertices whose distance was just improved, using a FIFO work queue instead of blindly sweeping every edge every round
variations.inputShape: Weighted graph as adjacency list, possibly with negative edges
variations.movement: Wavefront-like propagation driven by a work queue rather than fixed V-1 sweeps
variations.timeComplexity: O(V * E) worst case, often much faster in practice on sparse graphs
variations.recognizeIt: You need Bellman-Ford's correctness on negative edges, but the graph is sparse and a fixed full V-1 sweep every round feels wasteful.
variations.code:
from collections import deque

def spfa(num_vertices, adj, source):
    dist = [float('inf')] * num_vertices
    dist[source] = 0
    in_queue = [False] * num_vertices
    queue = deque([source])
    in_queue[source] = True

    while queue:
        u = queue.popleft()
        in_queue[u] = False
        for v, w in adj[u]:
            if dist[u] + w < dist[v]:
                dist[v] = dist[u] + w
                if not in_queue[v]:
                    queue.append(v)
                    in_queue[v] = True

    return dist
variations.keyInsight: A vertex only needs to be re-relaxed when one of its incoming distances actually changed -- tracking that with a queue skips most of the redundant work full Bellman-Ford repeats.

patterns.name: Floyd-Warshall Algorithm (All-Pairs Shortest Path)
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: Floyd-Warshall computes the shortest distance between every pair of vertices in a graph, not just from a single source. It works by considering each vertex k in turn as a possible 'intermediate' stop, and for every pair (i, j), checking whether routing through k gives a shorter path than the best known so far. After allowing all V vertices as intermediates, the distance matrix holds the true shortest path between every pair. It handles negative edge weights (but not negative cycles) and is simple to implement with triple-nested loops instead of a heap.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Finding shortest distances between every pair of vertices at once
Small dense graphs where computing all-pairs distances individually via Dijkstra would be repetitive
Detecting negative cycles anywhere in the graph
Computing transitive closure / reachability between all node pairs
Finding the city that minimizes the number of reachable neighbors within a distance threshold

steps: Initialize a V x V distance matrix: dist[i][j] = 0 if i == j, the edge weight if a direct edge exists, otherwise infinity.
For each vertex k from 0 to V-1, treat it as a candidate intermediate vertex.
For every pair (i, j), check if dist[i][k] + dist[k][j] is smaller than the current dist[i][j].
If so, update dist[i][j] to dist[i][k] + dist[k][j].
After the outer loop finishes for all k, dist[i][j] holds the true shortest distance between every pair.
Optionally, check the diagonal: if any dist[i][i] < 0, a negative cycle exists.

variations.name: Standard All-Pairs Shortest Path
variations.summary: Triple-nested loop building the full pairwise distance matrix by trying every vertex as an intermediate
variations.inputShape: Dense or small graph, given as edge list or adjacency matrix
variations.movement: Layered by intermediate vertex k, then scans every (i, j) pair per layer
variations.recognizeIt: Small number of vertices (roughly V <= 400-500) and the question needs distances between many/all pairs, not just from one source.
variations.keyInsight: The order of the loops matters critically -- k must be the outermost loop, since dist[i][k] and dist[k][j] must already reflect paths that only use intermediates from {0..k-1} before vertex k itself is allowed as a stop.

variations.name: Floyd-Warshall with path reconstruction
variations.summary: Tracks a 'next' matrix alongside distances so the actual shortest path between any pair can be rebuilt afterward
variations.inputShape: Dense graph, edge list or adjacency matrix
variations.movement: Same triple loop, with an extra matrix update whenever a distance improves
variations.recognizeIt: The problem needs the actual route between two given nodes, not just the distance, and multiple such queries are expected.
variations.keyInsight: nxt[i][j] only ever needs to store 'the first hop from i toward j' -- when a shorter path through k is found, copying nxt[i][k] (not nxt[k][j]) correctly redirects i's first step without needing to store the whole path.

patterns.name: Kruskal's Algorithm
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: Kruskal's algorithm builds a minimum spanning tree by sorting all edges by weight ascending, then greedily adding each edge to the tree as long as it doesn't create a cycle. A Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union) structure is used to efficiently check whether two vertices are already connected, and to merge them once an edge is accepted. The process stops once V-1 edges have been added, since that's exactly enough to connect V vertices without a cycle.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Connecting all nodes in a network at minimum total edge cost
Finding the minimum cost to connect all cities/computers/points
Building a minimum spanning tree from a sparse edge list
Finding the maximum bottleneck edge weight to keep a graph connected
Clustering problems where MST edges represent natural groupings

steps: Sort all edges in ascending order of weight.
Initialize a Union-Find structure with each vertex in its own set.
Initialize an empty list for MST edges and a running total cost of 0.
For each edge (u, v, w) in sorted order, check if u and v are already in the same set.
If they are in different sets, add the edge to the MST, add w to the total cost, and union the two sets.
If they are already in the same set, skip the edge since adding it would create a cycle.
Stop once V-1 edges have been added, or after scanning all edges.
Return the MST edges and/or the total cost.

variations.name: Standard Kruskal's with Union-Find
variations.summary: Sort edges globally, then greedily accept each edge whose endpoints are in different DSU sets
variations.inputShape: Sparse weighted graph, given as an edge list
variations.movement: Global sweep over edges in increasing weight order, independent of any starting vertex
variations.recognizeIt: The graph is given as a plain edge list (not adjacency structure) and is sparse -- sorting edges once is cheap relative to E.
variations.keyInsight: Greedily taking the globally cheapest edge that doesn't form a cycle is always safe -- this is the 'cut property' of MSTs: the lightest edge crossing any cut must belong to some MST.

variations.name: Kruskal's for max-bottleneck / threshold connectivity
variations.summary: Same sorted-edge greedy union process, but used to find the minimum weight needed to fully connect the graph rather than the total MST cost
variations.inputShape: Weighted edge list where the question asks about a threshold or bottleneck edge
variations.movement: Same ascending sweep, but the answer of interest is the last accepted edge's weight, not the sum
variations.recognizeIt: The question asks for 'minimum possible maximum edge weight' to keep the graph connected, or similar bottleneck phrasing, rather than total cost.
variations.keyInsight: Since edges are processed in increasing order, the weight of the very last edge needed to fully connect all components is guaranteed to be the smallest possible 'maximum edge' over any connecting edge set.

patterns.name: Prim's Algorithm
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: Prim's algorithm builds a minimum spanning tree by growing a single connected tree outward one vertex at a time, always adding the cheapest edge that connects a vertex already in the tree to one that isn't. A min-heap tracks the cheapest known edge to each vertex outside the tree, similar in spirit to Dijkstra but relaxing based on a single edge weight rather than accumulated distance. It's most efficient on dense graphs where an adjacency-matrix or adjacency-list plus heap approach avoids sorting all edges upfront.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Building a minimum spanning tree starting from a specific vertex outward
Dense graphs where Kruskal's global edge sort would be less efficient
Minimum cost to connect all points/nodes when the graph is naturally adjacency-based
Network design problems minimizing total cable/pipe/wire length
Problems where the graph is implicit (e.g. a grid of points) and edges are generated on the fly

steps: Pick an arbitrary starting vertex and mark it as in the MST.
Push all edges from the starting vertex onto a min-heap, keyed by weight.
While the heap is not empty and the MST doesn't yet have V-1 edges, pop the cheapest edge.
If the edge's destination vertex is already in the MST, skip it (it would create a cycle).
Otherwise, add the edge to the MST, mark the destination vertex as included, and add its weight to the total cost.
Push all edges from the newly added vertex to still-outside vertices onto the heap.
Repeat until V-1 edges have been added; return the MST edges and/or total cost.
stepByStepAlgorithm.code:
import heapq

def prim_mst(num_vertices, edges):
    graph = [[] for _ in range(num_vertices)]
    for u, v, w in edges:
        graph[u].append((w, v))
        graph[v].append((w, u))

    in_mst = [False] * num_vertices
    in_mst[0] = True
    heap = list(graph[0])
    heapq.heapify(heap)

    total_cost = 0
    edges_used = 0

    while heap and edges_used < num_vertices - 1:
        weight, node = heapq.heappop(heap)
        if in_mst[node]:
            continue  # would create a cycle
        in_mst[node] = True
        total_cost += weight
        edges_used += 1
        for next_weight, neighbor in graph[node]:
            if not in_mst[neighbor]:
                heapq.heappush(heap, (next_weight, neighbor))

    return total_cost if edges_used == num_vertices - 1 else -1

variations.name: Lazy Prim's with min-heap
variations.summary: Pushes every candidate edge onto the heap and simply skips stale entries when popped, rather than updating them in place
variations.movement: Grows a single tree outward from a start vertex, always taking the globally cheapest frontier edge
variations.recognizeIt: Straightforward MST construction from an adjacency-list graph, and simplicity of implementation matters more than shaving log factors.
variations.code:
import heapq

def prim_mst(num_vertices, edges):
    graph = [[] for _ in range(num_vertices)]
    for u, v, w in edges:
        graph[u].append((w, v))
        graph[v].append((w, u))

    in_mst = [False] * num_vertices
    in_mst[0] = True
    heap = list(graph[0])
    heapq.heapify(heap)

    total_cost = 0
    edges_used = 0

    while heap and edges_used < num_vertices - 1:
        weight, node = heapq.heappop(heap)
        if in_mst[node]:
            continue
        in_mst[node] = True
        total_cost += weight
        edges_used += 1
        for next_weight, neighbor in graph[node]:

variations.keyInsight: Just like Dijkstra's stale-entry skip, letting outdated heap entries sit and get discarded later is simpler and just as correct as trying to decrease-key them in place.

variations.name: Eager Prim's with min-distance array
variations.summary: Maintains a 'best known edge weight to reach each vertex' array and updates it in place, similar to Dijkstra's distance-array style, instead of pushing duplicate heap entries
variations.inputShape: Dense weighted graph, adjacency list or matrix
variations.movement: Same outward tree growth, but frontier costs are tracked and improved directly rather than via stale-heap-entry skipping
variations.timeComplexity: O(V^2) with array, or O(E log V) with an indexed/decrease-key heap
variations.recognizeIt: Dense graphs (E close to V^2) where an O(V^2) array-scan approach outperforms a heap due to fewer overheads, or when memory for many heap entries is a concern.
variations.keyInsight: On a dense graph, scanning all V vertices to find the minimum V times (O(V^2) total) avoids the log-factor and heap-management overhead that dominates when E is close to V^2.

patterns.name: Detect Cycle in Undirected Graph (Union-Find)
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: Instead of DFS/BFS parent-tracking, this approach detects a cycle in an undirected graph by processing edges one at a time and using a Disjoint Set Union structure to track which vertices are already connected. For each edge (u, v), if u and v are already in the same set, adding this edge would connect two already-connected vertices, meaning a cycle exists. Otherwise, the two sets are merged (unioned). Path compression and union by rank keep each operation nearly constant time.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Detecting a cycle in an undirected graph using an iterative, non-recursive approach
Finding the one redundant edge that creates a cycle in a graph
Incrementally checking connectivity as edges are added one at a time
Counting connected components while processing an edge stream
Determining whether a set of provinces/friend-groups are all connected

steps: Initialize a parent array where each vertex is its own parent (its own set), and a rank array of all zeros.
For each edge (u, v) in the edge list, find the root of u's set and the root of v's set.
If the two roots are the same, u and v are already connected -- this edge creates a cycle, so report it and stop (or record it).
Otherwise, union the two sets: attach the smaller-rank tree under the larger-rank tree's root (or by size).
Use path compression during find so future lookups on these vertices are faster.
Continue processing all edges; if no edge ever triggers the same-root case, the graph is acyclic.

variations.name: DSU with path compression + union by rank
variations.summary: The optimized standard DSU: path compression flattens trees during find, union by rank keeps trees shallow during union
variations.inputShape: Undirected edge list, processed one edge at a time
variations.movement: Iterative, no recursion -- one pass over the edge list
variations.timeComplexity: O(E * alpha(V)) -- alpha is the inverse Ackermann function, effectively constant
variations.recognizeIt: Edges are given as a flat list (not needing full graph traversal), and either an iterative solution or incremental connectivity checks are wanted.
variations.keyInsight: Path compression and union by rank each independently improve performance, but combined they give near-constant amortized time per operation -- one of the few 'almost free' optimizations in algorithm design.

variations.name: DSU with union by size
variations.summary: Same idea as union by rank, but tracks the actual number of elements in each set to decide which tree to attach under which
variations.inputShape: Undirected edge list or dynamic connectivity queries
variations.movement: Iterative, same as rank-based version
variations.recognizeIt: The problem also asks for the size of the connected component(s) directly (e.g. largest connected group), where tracking size is directly useful rather than an abstract rank.
variations.keyInsight: Tracking size instead of rank costs nothing extra but directly answers 'how big is this component' without a separate counting pass.

patterns.name: Bipartite Graph Check
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: A graph is bipartite if its vertices can be split into two groups such that every edge connects a vertex in one group to a vertex in the other, meaning no edge connects two vertices in the same group. This is checked by 2-coloring the graph via BFS or DFS: start each unvisited vertex with color 0, and whenever you move to a neighbor, assign it the opposite color. If a neighbor is already visited and has the same color as the current vertex, the graph cannot be 2-colored, so it isn't bipartite.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Checking whether a graph's vertices can be split into two independent groups
Verifying if a graph contains no odd-length cycles
Matching problems (e.g. job assignment) that require an underlying bipartite structure
Determining if people/items can be divided into two non-conflicting teams/sets
Validating graph coloring constraints with exactly 2 colors

steps: Create a color array initialized to -1 (uncolored) for every vertex.
For each uncolored vertex, start a BFS assigning it color 0 and pushing it onto a queue.
While the queue is not empty, pop a vertex and look at each neighbor.
If a neighbor is uncolored, assign it the opposite color of the current vertex and push it onto the queue.
If a neighbor is already colored the same as the current vertex, the graph is not bipartite -- return false immediately.
Repeat for all components; if no conflict is ever found, the graph is bipartite -- return true.

variations.name: BFS 2-coloring
variations.summary: Iteratively colors vertices level by level using a queue, flipping color at each step
variations.inputShape: Undirected graph, possibly disconnected, adjacency list
variations.movement: Level-order outward from each unvisited component's start vertex
variations.recognizeIt: Straightforward yes/no bipartite check, or when avoiding recursion depth issues on large/deep graphs matters.
variations.keyInsight: Checking each disconnected component separately is essential -- a graph is bipartite overall only if every individual component is independently 2-colorable.

variations.name: DFS 2-coloring
variations.summary: Same 2-coloring rule, applied recursively depth-first instead of level by level
variations.movement: Depth-first; color assigned on entry, conflict checked against already-colored neighbors
variations.recognizeIt: A recursive formulation is more natural for the problem, or the bipartite check is combined with another DFS-based computation.

patterns.name: Multi-Source BFS
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: Multi-source BFS starts the traversal from several source vertices simultaneously instead of just one, by pushing all of them onto the queue at distance 0 before the main loop begins. This correctly computes, for every vertex, the shortest distance to the NEAREST of the sources -- not the distance from any single one -- because all sources expand outward in lockstep, one BFS layer at a time. It's especially common on grid problems where multiple starting cells (e.g. all rotten oranges, all initially infected cells) spread outward together.
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Finding the shortest distance from any of several starting points to every other cell/node
Simulating simultaneous spread from multiple sources (fire, infection, rot) across a grid
Finding the cell farthest from all given source cells
Computing 'distance to nearest X' for every cell in a grid (nearest 0, nearest guard, etc.)
Determining the time until all reachable cells are affected

steps: Initialize a distance/time array (or grid) with infinity for every cell.
Find all source cells and push each of them onto a queue with distance 0, setting their distance to 0.
While the queue is not empty, pop a cell and look at its unvisited neighbors.
For each unvisited neighbor, set its distance to the current cell's distance + 1 and push it onto the queue.
Continue until the queue is empty -- every cell now holds its shortest distance to the nearest original source.
If the problem asks for a specific quantity (e.g. max distance, or whether all cells were reached), derive it from the final distance array.

variations.name: Grid simultaneous spread (rotting/infection)
variations.summary: All initial 'affected' cells are seeded into the queue at once; BFS naturally simulates them spreading outward in sync, one minute/step per layer
variations.inputShape: 2D grid with multiple marked source cells
variations.movement: Simultaneous outward expansion in lockstep from all sources
variations.recognizeIt: Multiple starting cells that all 'spread' at the same rate simultaneously, and the question asks for total time/minutes until spread stops or covers everything.
variations.keyInsight: Seeding every source cell into the queue before the first pop, all at the same starting distance, is what makes the spread happen in true simultaneous lockstep rather than one source finishing before another starts.

variations.name: Maximize distance to nearest source
variations.summary: Same multi-source BFS distance computation, but the answer of interest is the maximum value in the resulting distance array
variations.inputShape: 2D grid with source cells scattered among empty cells
variations.movement: Same simultaneous outward BFS
variations.recognizeIt: The problem asks to find the cell that is 'as far as possible' from all given source cells (e.g. 'maximize distance to the nearest' phrasing).
variations.keyInsight: Processing the queue in whole-layer batches (using the current queue length) instead of one cell at a time lets you track 'distance' as a simple layer counter rather than storing it per cell.

patterns.name: 0-1 BFS
patterns.whatIsThisPattern: 0-1 BFS finds shortest paths in graphs where every edge weight is either 0 or 1, without needing a full priority queue like Dijkstra. It uses a double-ended queue (deque): when relaxing an edge of weight 0, the neighbor is pushed to the FRONT of the deque (since it doesn't increase the path cost); when relaxing an edge of weight 1, the neighbor is pushed to the BACK. This keeps the deque ordered by distance at all times, giving Dijkstra-like correctness in linear time instead of O(E log V).
whatProblemsDoesItSolve: Shortest path in graphs with only 0 and 1 edge weights
Grid problems where moving in some directions is free and others cost 1 (e.g. minimum obstacle removal)
Minimum number of edge-direction flips to enable a path (treating reversed edges as weight 1)
Problems disguised as weighted shortest path but the weights only ever take two values
Faster alternative to Dijkstra when weights are constrained to {0, 1}

steps: Initialize a distance array with infinity for every vertex except the source, which is 0.
Initialize an empty deque and push the source vertex onto it.
While the deque is not empty, pop a vertex from the FRONT.
For each neighbor reachable via an edge of weight w (0 or 1), compute the new distance as dist[current] + w.
If this new distance improves the neighbor's recorded distance, update it.
If the edge weight was 0, push the neighbor onto the FRONT of the deque; if the edge weight was 1, push it onto the BACK.
Continue until the deque is empty; the distance array now holds shortest distances from the source.

variations.name: Standard 0-1 BFS with deque
variations.summary: Push 0-weight edges to the front and 1-weight edges to the back of a deque, replacing the need for a heap
variations.inputShape: Graph with only two distinct edge weights, 0 and 1
variations.movement: Deque stays sorted by distance implicitly, expanding in near-BFS order
variations.recognizeIt: Edge weights in the problem are explicitly restricted to exactly two values, 0 and 1 -- a strong signal against using full Dijkstra.
variations.keyInsight: Pushing 0-weight neighbors to the front preserves the invariant that the deque's front-to-back order always corresponds to non-decreasing distance, which is exactly what a priority queue would guarantee but achieved here in O(1) per push.

variations.name: Grid obstacle elimination via 0-1 BFS
variations.summary: Applies 0-1 BFS to an implicit grid graph where moving onto an open cell costs 0 and moving onto (or through) an obstacle costs 1
variations.inputShape: 2D grid with some cells marked as obstacles/walls
variations.movement: Same deque-based expansion, with neighbors generated from grid coordinates
variations.recognizeIt: A grid problem asking for the minimum number of walls/obstacles to remove (or directions to change) to reach a destination -- effectively a 0/1 cost per cell.
variations.keyInsight: Reframing 'minimum walls to remove/signs to change' as edge weights of 0 (matches existing direction) or 1 (must override it) turns a seemingly complex grid puzzle into a direct 0-1 BFS application.
